Haberkorn Clinical Cooperation Unit Nuclear Medicine, German Cancer Research Centre, Heidelberg, Germany Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2014) 41:11–20 DOI 10.1007/s00259-013-2525-5 respectively. In addition, tumour to background ratios were calculated. Results A total of 78 lesions characteristic for PC were detect- ed in 32 patients using 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT and 56 lesions were detected in 26 patients using choline PET/CT. The higher detection rate in 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT was statistically signif- icant (p =0.04). In five patients no lesion was found with both methods. All lesions detected by18F-fluoromethylcholine PET/ CT were also seen by 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT. In 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT SUV max was clearly (>10 %) higher in 62 of 78 lesions (79.1 %) and the tumour to background ratio was clearly (>10 %) higher in 74 of 78 lesions (94.9 %) when compared to 18F-fluoromethylcholine PET/CT. Conclusion 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT can detect lesions charac- teristic for PC with improved contrast when compared to standard 18F-fluoromethylcholine PET/CT, especially at low PSA levels. Keywords Prostate cancer . PET/CT . Positron emission tomography . PSMA . Choline Introduction Prostate cancer (PC) is the second most frequent cancer and the sixth leading cause of cancer death in men worldwide [1]. One of the key issues of this tumour entity is to detect recurrent disease. To date this is a major challenge for all conventional imaging modalities [ 2]. Although choline- based positron emission tomography (PET)/CT is widely used for this purpose, there have been numerous studies reporting a low sensitivity and specificity, especially at low prostate- specific antigen (PSA) levels [3–13]. Consequently, improved imaging of PC is necessary. One novel promising method is PET

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: ed for this purpose, there have been numerous studies reporting a low sensitivity and specificity, especially at low prostate- specific antigen (PSA) levels [3–13]. Consequently, improved imaging of PC is necessary. One novel promising method is PET imaging with 18F-FACBC, a new synthetic amino acid. Recent evaluations by Nanni et al. indicate that this tracer might be superior when compared to choline PET/CT [ 14]. In addition, prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) recently has received increased attention [ 15–19]. This cell surface protein is significantly overexpressed in PC cells when compared to other PSMA-expressing tissues such as kidney, proximal small intestine or salivary glands [ 20, 21]. It there- fore provides a promising target for PC-specific imaging and therapy [22]. Recently methods have been developed to label PSMA ligands with 68Ga, 99mTc and radioiodine enabling their use for PET or single photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) imaging and therapy [15, 16, 22–24]. Our initial experience with PET/CT using Glu-NH-CO-NH-Lys-(Ahx)-[ 68Ga(HBED-CC)] (68Ga-PSMA) as a68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand suggests that this novel tracer can detect PC relapses and metastases with high contrast by binding to the extracellular domain of PSMA, followed by internalization [25, 26]. The aim of our study was to compare this novel tracer with standard choline-based PET/ CT in the diagnostics of patients presenting a biochemical recurrence of the disease. Materials and methods Patient characteristics For this study we originally selected 38 male patients who underwent both choline-based PET/CT and68Ga-PSMA PET/ CT within a time window of 30 days. One of the patients was deleted from the study as the histological evaluation revealed multiple metastatic and converging lym

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: ally selected 38 male patients who underwent both choline-based PET/CT and68Ga-PSMA PET/ CT within a time window of 30 days. One of the patients was deleted from the study as the histological evaluation revealed multiple metastatic and converging lymph node packages. This precludes radiological differentiation of individual lymph nodes and a meaningful quantitative comparison of lymph node detection rate between choline and PSMA PET/CT. Therefore, the total number of patients analysed in this study was 37. In all cases there was suspected progressive disease following prior conventional treatment of PC (e.g. hormone therapy, chemotherapy, radiation therapy and/or surgery). All patients signed a written informed consent form for the purpose of anonymized evaluation and publication of their data. The quantity of patients presented in this study reflects our data collected during the past 3 years. Follow-up could be conducted in 33 of 37 patients up until the time this manu- script was submitted. With the exception of patients 26 and 29 (organiza- tional reasons), all patients were first investigated by 18F-fluoromethylcholine PET/CT. This is currently the method of choice in routine clinical practice. However, due to unsatisfying imaging results of choline PET/CT and to evaluate possible treatment with 131I-labelled PSMA ligands (therapy data not yet published), further investigation using 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT was suggested. This additional PET scan was conducted only in case both patients and their referring physicians gave their consent. All reported investigations were conducted in accordance with the Helsinki Declaration and with our national regula- tions. This study was approved by the Ethics Committee of the University of Heidelberg (permit S-321/2012). Patient charact

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: consent. All reported investigations were conducted in accordance with the Helsinki Declaration and with our national regula- tions. This study was approved by the Ethics Committee of the University of Heidelberg (permit S-321/2012). Patient characteristics are summarized in Table 1. Twenty-eight patients had previously undergone prostatecto- my (Table 1,p a t i e n t s1 ,3–4, 7–8, 10–16, 19, 22–23 and 25 – 37), whereas nine were treated with prior radiation therapy and androgen deprivation without surgical removal of the prostate. The average age was 69.3±7.1 years (range 57 –85, median 70.0) with a mean Gleason score (GSC) of 7.4±1.1 (range 5–9, median 7.0) and a mean PSA level of 11.1±24.1 ng/ml (range 0.01–116, median 4.0 ng/ml). PSA was measured in blood samples taken at the time of the first PET/CT scan. The average time between both investigations was 12.1±8.4 days (range 1– 30, median 11.0). 12 Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2014) 41:11–20 Fifteen patients (3, 5–14, 16–17 and 19–20) of the present study have coincidentally been analysed in a different study evaluating the biodistribution of the PSMA tracer in humans [26]. This concordance was not intentional. The authors are convinced that these patients had to be integrated into the present manuscript instead of being excluded from the novel study. Their inclusion improves data quality and confirms the findings of this study. Imaging 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT was obtained with the 68Ga-labelled HBED-CC conjugate of the PSMA-specific pharmacophore Glu-NH-CO-NH-Lys that was synthesized as described pre- viously [16]. 68Ga3+ was obtained from a 68Ge/68Ga radionu- clide generator and complexed with the HBED-CC conjugate as previously published [ 16, 17]. The final product was Ta bl e 1 Patient characteristics Grey fields: pat

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: hesized as described pre- viously [16]. 68Ga3+ was obtained from a 68Ge/68Ga radionu- clide generator and complexed with the HBED-CC conjugate as previously published [ 16, 17]. The final product was Ta bl e 1 Patient characteristics Grey fields: patients without pathological tracer uptake in both PSMA-ligand and Choline based PET/CT Framed numbers: patients without pathological findings in Choline-PET/CT only Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2014) 41:11–20 13 formulated in isotonic phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) with subsequent sterile filtration. The radiolabelling and purifica- tion of the PSMA ligand was done using an automated mod- ule. As a consequence, the radiochemical yield was not deter- mined regularly. Typically, the labelling efficiency (radio- chemical yield) is >98 % as determined by module validation. The 68Ga-PSMA complex solution was applied to patients via an intravenous bolus (mean 139.6±46.3 MBq, range 59 – 263 MBq, median 132 MBq). Targeted 68Ga-PSMA was 2 MBq/kg. V ariation of injected radiotracer activity was caused by the short half-life of 68Ga and variable elution efficiencies obtained during the lifetime of the 68Ge/68Ga radionuclide generator. However, in our experience with PSMA PET/CT during the last 3 years, all injected activities were sufficient in detecting PC (see also in the “Discussion”). All injections contained 2 nmol PSMA ligand resulting in a median specific radioactivity of 66 GBq/μmol. Choline-based PET/CT was performed with 18F-labelled fluoromethylcholine (IASOcholine®, Iason, Graz, Austria). The production was according to common radiopharmaceuti- cal standards and regulations. The choline solution was ap- plied to patients via an intravenous bolus (mean 241.6± 59.4 MBq, range 114–374 MBq, median 237 MBq). Targeted choline acti

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: Austria). The production was according to common radiopharmaceuti- cal standards and regulations. The choline solution was ap- plied to patients via an intravenous bolus (mean 241.6± 59.4 MBq, range 114–374 MBq, median 237 MBq). Targeted choline activity was 3 MBq/kg. A non-contrast-enhanced CT scan was performed 1 h post tracer injection using the following parameters: slice thickness of 5 mm, increment of 0.8 mm, soft tissue reconstruction kernel, 130 keV and 80 mAs. Immediately after CT scanning, a whole-body PET was acquired in 3D (matrix 164×164). For each bed position (16.2 cm, overlapping scale 4.2 cm) we used 4-min acquisition time with a 15.5-cm field of view (FOV). The emission data were corrected for randoms, scatter and decay. Reconstruction was conducted with an ordered subset expectation maximization (OSEM) algorithm with 2 iterations/8 subsets and Gauss-filtered to a transaxial resolu- tion of 5 mm at full-width at half-maximum (FWHM). Atten- uation correction was performed using the low-dose non- enhanced CT data. PET and CT were performed using the same protocol for every patient on a Biograph 6 PET/CT scanner (Siemens, Erlangen, Germany). Image analysis Image analysis was performed using an appropriate worksta- tion and software (Syngo TrueD, Siemens, Erlangen, Germa- ny). Two board-certified specialists in nuclear medicine with 8 and 9 years of clinical experiences read all data sets indepen- dently and resolved any disagreements by consensus. Choline and PSMA PET/CT were analysed in a randomized fashion. Lesions that were visually considered as suggestive of PC were counted and analysed with respect to their localization (local relapses, lymph node, bone and soft tissue metastases) and to their maximum standardized uptake values (SUV max) as it is co

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: Lesions that were visually considered as suggestive of PC were counted and analysed with respect to their localization (local relapses, lymph node, bone and soft tissue metastases) and to their maximum standardized uptake values (SUV max) as it is common practice in daily routine. SUVmax was chosen due to its higher reproducibility between different investiga- tors when compared to SUV mean. The latter one is always dependent on the volume of interest (VOI) drawn by the investigator, whereas SUVmax is independent [26]. For calculation of the SUV , circular regions of interest were drawn around areas with focally increased uptake in transaxial slices and automatically adapted to a three-dimensional vol- ume of interest at a 70 % isocontour. In the case of suspicious focal tracer uptake in one investigation and simultaneous virtually inconspicuous finding in the same area using the corresponding different PET/CT technique, SUV was mea- sured in the inconspicuous areas as well. SUV max of the same lesions in both PSMA ligand- and fluoromethylcholine-based PET/CT were defined as clearly less, equal or clearly more with intensity differences of ≤10 %, be- tween−10 and +10 % or >10 %, respectively. SUV values of the same lesions in both PSMA liga nd- and fluoromethylcholine- based PET/CT as well as their ratio to background signal (in SUV max) were statistically analysed using a Wilcoxon signed rank test. When analysing the contrast of lesions with pathological tracer uptake and therefore visually highly suggestive of PC (characteristic for PC), several background tissues corre- sponding to the localization of the lesions were selected. This method is more accurate and better reflects the contrasting ability of the imaging modality in the region of interest when compared to t

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: ristic for PC), several background tissues corre- sponding to the localization of the lesions were selected. This method is more accurate and better reflects the contrasting ability of the imaging modality in the region of interest when compared to the selection of one general background tissue that might show differing background uptake than the region of interest. However, particularly in cases of a variety of metastases the selection of multiple backgrounds is not always feasible. For our study we chose the background according to the following algorithm: In cases of bone metastases in the vertebral column, of local relapses, of lung metastases and of liver metastases, we selected adjacent normal tissue as background. In cases of non-vertebral bone metastases, we selected the contralateral normal bone tissue. In cases of soft tissue metastases and of lymph node metastases, the gluteal musculature was selected as background. The selection of two different backgrounds in cases of bone metastases (vertebral and non-vertebral) as mentioned above is due to the well-known fact that the background signal in choline-based PET imaging is higher in the vertebral column when compared to other skeletal structures (e.g. Fig. 4d). The statistical differences between the background signal of the vertebral column and other parts of the skeletal system in choline PET/CT are described in the “Results”. Statistical analysis For statistical analysis, Excel 2010 (Microsoft, Redmond, W A, USA) and SigmaPlot version 11 software (Systat 14 Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2014) 41:11–20 Software, Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) were used. Significance of differences was evaluated by: 1. Two-sided Wilcoxon signed rank tests for tumour uptake and contrast in both PET/CT methods. 2. Two-sided pairedt tests

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2014) 41:11–20 Software, Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) were used. Significance of differences was evaluated by: 1. Two-sided Wilcoxon signed rank tests for tumour uptake and contrast in both PET/CT methods. 2. Two-sided pairedt tests to evaluate differences concerning the background signal between choline- and PSMA-based PET/CT. 3. Two-sided unpaired two-samplet tests to evaluate differ- ences concerning GSC and applied radioactivity between groups with and without pathological uptakes. 4. Two-sided Mann–Whitney tests to evaluate differences concerning PSA values between groups with and without pathological uptakes. 5. Two-sided McNemar test to analyse whether 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT detects significantly more lesions characteristic for PC when compared to choline-based PET/CT. In all cases a p value of <0.05 was considered statistically significant. Furthermore, regression analysis between PSA and SUV max was done for both investigations. Results There were no adverse or clinically detectable pharmacolog- ical effects in any of the patients after injection of both tracers. In 32 of 37 (86.5 %) patients at least 1 lesion characteristic for PC was detected in 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT. By contrast, only 26 of 37 (70.3 %) patients presented with pathological findings in 18F-fluoromethylcholine PET/CT. Using 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT 78 lesions characteristic for PC were detected in 32 patients and using18F-fluoromethylcholine PET/CT 56 lesions were detected in 26 patients. The higher detection rate in 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT was significant (McNemar test, p =0.04). In five patients no lesion was found with both methods (Table1). With PSA values of 2.82 ng/ml and less, at least one lesion characteristic for PC was identified in 68.8 % of patients, while all patients presented with pathol

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: (McNemar test, p =0.04). In five patients no lesion was found with both methods (Table1). With PSA values of 2.82 ng/ml and less, at least one lesion characteristic for PC was identified in 68.8 % of patients, while all patients presented with pathological lesions at PSA values greater than 2.82 ng/ml. Using the same threshold for 18 F-fluoromethylcholine PET/CT, 43.8 % of patients presented with at least one lesion at PSA levels of 2.82 ng/ ml and less. In addition, 90.5 % of the patients presented with lesions at levels greater than 2.82 ng/ml. All lesions detected by 18F-fluoromethylcholine PET/CT were also seen in 68Ga- PSMA PET/CT. Figure1b demonstrates the tumour to background ratio which was clearly (>10 %) higher in 74 of 78 lesions (=94.9 %, which was significant, p <0.001) when using 68Ga-PSMA. The four remaining lesions in two different patients presented with higher ratios in 18F-fluoromethylcholine PET/CT (Table 1,p a t i e n t s7 and 27, Fig. 1, lesions 7 and 50–52). Concerning both SUV max of lesions and their signal to background ratio, the most significant differences between the two imaging methods were observed in lymph node metastases foll

text: T/CT (Table 1,p a t i e n t s7 and 27, Fig. 1, lesions 7 and 50–52). Concerning both SUV max of lesions and their signal to background ratio, the most significant differences between the two imaging methods were observed in lymph node metastases followed by bone metastases, local relapses and soft tissue metastases as demonstrated by Fig. 1 and Tables 2–3 in supplementary data. Figures 2, 3 and 4 demonstrate selected examples of the improved contrast when using the PSMA ligand. In all selected background tissues, tracer uptake (as mea- sured with SUV max) was significantly lower in 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT than in 18F-fluoromethylcholine PET/CT (two-sided paired t tests): p <0.001 in gluteal musculature, p <0. 001 in vertebral bones, p <0.001 in non-vertebral bones, p =0.014 in lungs and p <0.001 in the liver. Specific characteristics of patients with and without patho- logical findings in 18F-fluoromethylcholine PET/CT are de- tailed in Table 4 (supplementary data). Between these two groups, the mean PSA level was significantly lower (two-sided Mann–Whitney test,p =0.009) and GSC significantly higher in the group without pathological findings (t test, p =0.038). The injected dosage was not significantly higher in the group with pathological findings (t test p =0.87). Specific characteristics of patients with and without patho- logical findings in 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT are also detailed in Table 4 (supplementary data). Between these two groups, injected dosage ( t test, p =0.76) and PSA level (two-sided Mann–Whitney test, p =0.096) were not significantly lower in the group without pathological findings. The GSC was not significantly higher in the group without pathological findings (t test, p =0.42). There was no relation in the regression analysis between PSA and SUV values in both

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: not significantly lower in the group without pathological findings. The GSC was not significantly higher in the group without pathological findings (t test, p =0.42). There was no relation in the regression analysis between PSA and SUV values in both choline- and PSMA-based PET/CT (raw data not shown). Seven patients with pathological radiotracer uptake in 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT were further investigated by biopsy or surgery (Table 1,p a t i e n t s1 1–13, 16 –17, 27 and 32). In all cases PC was confirmed. No false-positive or false-negative lesions were found in all of these cases. In addition, ten patients were treated by selected radiation therapy (patients 1, 3, 4, 6, 10, 21, 29–31 and 34). In all cases, PSA decreased significantly after radiation. One patient (35) was treated by radiation as well, but the first PSA evaluation after treatment was still pending at the time of manuscript submission. Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2014) 41:11–20 15 Three patients (18, 25 and 33) were treated with 131I- labelled PSMA ligands. After treatment, PSA decreased sig- nificantly as well. Like selective radiation, these therapies demonstrated also that the PSMA-positive lesions were me- tastases of PC. Eight patients (7, 8, 20, 23, 24, 26, 28 and 36) were treated with androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) only. In all cases, PSA decreased as well. Four patients were re- ferred for active surveillance (2, 5, 9 and 19) and four other patients were not available for follow-up (14, 15, 22 and 37). Further explanation of patients 4 and 36: after initial PET/ CT without pathological findings (part of this study), both patients were referred for a second PSMA PET/CT 1 year after the first investigation. In the case of patient 4, one nodular PSMA-positive lesion in the pelvis was found. This lesio

In the case of patient 4, one nodular PSMA-positive lesion in the pelvis was found. This lesion was retrospectively already existent in the first PET/CT although it was smaller and did not present with pathological tracer uptake. In the case of patient 36, multiple lymph node metastases were found in the second scan only. Therefore, the first scan was obviously false-negative in both cases. In summary, PSMA-positive lesions were proven to be PC by histology in seven cases. Discussion To date, the detection of lesions in the context of biochemical recurrence of PC is a major challenge for all imaging modalities including choline-based PET/CT [2–13]. The aim of this study was to compare the established 18F-fluoromethylcholine PET/ CT with a novel 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand. We retrospec- tively analysed 37 patients who underwent both 18F- fluoromethylcholine PET/CT and68Ga-PSMA PET/CT analy- sis within a time window of 30 days. In all cases there was suspected progressive disease following prior conventional treatment of PC. Most patients were initially referred for 18F- fluoromethylcholine PET/CT. However, due to reasons men- tioned before, further 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT was conducted. Theoretically the order of inve

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: ssive disease following prior conventional treatment of PC. Most patients were initially referred for 18F- fluoromethylcholine PET/CT. However, due to reasons men- tioned before, further 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT was conducted. Theoretically the order of investigations could introduce a bias due to possible tumour progression between the first and the second PET/CT. In reality however it is well known that PC usually presents with slow growth and noticeable changes within 30 days are very unlikely. Furthermore, the average time between both examinations was 11.9 days only. In the following, numerous essential results of this study are highlighted and discussed in detail to further demonstrate the superiority of 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT when compared to choline. In 86.5 % of the patients at least one lesion charac- teristic for PC was detected in 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT. This rate is similar to our previous data [ 26]. By contrast, only 70.3 % of the patients presented with pathological findings in 18F- fluoromethylcholine PET/CT which is similar to the data reported in the literature [ 3–13]. 18F-Fluoromethylcholine PET/CT did not reveal any suspicious lesions in 11 patients, while only 5 patients presented without any pathological findings in 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT. Especially at lower PSA levels,68Ga-PSMA PET/CT detect- ed more PC lesions when compared to choline. This was statis- tically significant as demonstrated in the results. These data are also similar to our previous report concerning this PSMA ligand [26]. Also the rates of choline are in agreement with the data reported in the literature [3–13]. Therefore, 68Ga-PSMA PET/ CT proved to be clearly superior in detecting PC lesions at low P S Al e v e l sw h e nc o m p a r e dt oc h o l i n e - b a s e dP E T / C T . Fig. 4 Patient 15. Red ar

Colour scales as automati- cally produced by the PET/CT machine 18 Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2014) 41:11–20 A significant advantage of 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT is that lesions characteristic for lymph node metastases frequently presented with very high contrast when compared to choline (Fig. 1 and Table 2). 18F-Fluoromethylcholine PET demon- strated low sensitivity in detecting lymph node metastases. This disadvantage of choline has also been reported in the literature [6, 7]. The superior contrast in 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT has also been demonstrated in most skeletal metastases and local re- lapses (Fig. 1,T a b l e s2 and 3 in supplementary data). This is even more

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: age of choline has also been reported in the literature [6, 7]. The superior contrast in 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT has also been demonstrated in most skeletal metastases and local re- lapses (Fig. 1,T a b l e s2 and 3 in supplementary data). This is even more significant than the differences of uptake values in both methods and illustrates one main drawback of choline- based PET: even at acceptable uptake values, a high back- ground signal frequently hampers the detection of lesions. Our study, therefore, demonstrates that metastases and recur- rent PC usually present with high contrast in 68Ga-PSMA PET/ CT contributing to a significantly improved detection of PC lesions even at low PSA levels when compared to 18F- fluoromethylcholine PET/CT. We hypothesize that detection rates in68Ga-PSMA PET/CT will increase with rising PSA levels and tumour size. Recently, Chondrogiannis et al. reported about a proportional correlation between trigger PSA and detection rate when using choline PET/CT [27]. On the other hand, there was no relation between PSA and SUV values in both choline- and P S M A - b a s e dP E T / C Ti no u rs t u d y .W i t hr e g a r dt oc h o l i n e ,o u r results are in agreement with the literature [ 28, 29]. For the PSMA tracer, however, no comparable data are available. Only four PC lesions in two patients presented with a better contrast in 18F-fluoromethylcholine PET/CT. Possible explana- tions for this finding are low injected activity of the PSMA ligand, previous therapies and di fferent biological properties such as PSMA expression, choline transport and phosphoryla- tion caused by tumour heterogeneity [21]. Both patients were injected with relatively low doses of 68Ga-PSMA. However, our experiences with this tracer suggest that similar or even lower doses m

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: as PSMA expression, choline transport and phosphoryla- tion caused by tumour heterogeneity [21]. Both patients were injected with relatively low doses of 68Ga-PSMA. However, our experiences with this tracer suggest that similar or even lower doses may be suitable to identify lesions with high target to background contrast [26]. As there were no further differences regarding other parameters, it is more probable that PSMA expression in these lesions is lo w, whereas choline transport and choline kinase activity are high. Although further analyses are needed to confirm these find- ings and to define sensitivity andspecificity, our data suggest an ability of the presented PSMA ligand to detect low differentiated PC lesions as well (low PSA levels despite multiple metastases and high initial GSC, e.g. patients 12 and 15). On the other hand, evidence exists for a low choline uptake in patients with poorly differentiated PC. In such cases 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) PET/CT can be used to improve the detection of lesions [30]. In contrast, PSMA expression is usually higher in lesions with higher GSC than in lesions with lower GSC [31]. The presented PSMA ligand, therefore, might be useful to reduce multiple investigations. Although PSMA-negative PC seems to be rare [21, 32], we cannot exclude the possibility of false-negative PET/CT imaging in patients without pathological findings. One other aspect is the fact that in patients investigated by histology there were no false-negative or false-positive findings indicat- ing a high sensitivity and specificity of the PSMA ligand. This is similar to our experiences with different patients outside this study examined only with the PSMA ligand who were inves- tigated by biopsy (data not yet published). Another aspect is that in case

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: ensitivity and specificity of the PSMA ligand. This is similar to our experiences with different patients outside this study examined only with the PSMA ligand who were inves- tigated by biopsy (data not yet published). Another aspect is that in cases of multiple metastases or metastases with difficult access, biopsy is neither indicated nor ethical. However, mul- tiple other patients who were treated by selective radiation and 131I-PSMA ligands followed by PSA decrease demonstrated sufficiently that the PSMA-positive lesions were indeed PC. With the exception of sensitivity and specificity, the most significant aspects of our study fulfilled the STARD criteria. However, our study simply followed the experiences of daily routine and therefore might be of relevance until results that conform 100 % with STARD are available in the future. One additional aspect of the presented 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand is the fact that no (cost-intensive) cyclotron is needed. 68Ga can be extracted from a commercially available 68Ge/68Ga radionuclide generator. In contrast, radiolabelling choline tracers requires isotopes produced by a cyclotron (e.g. 18Fo r 11C). Further analyses are also required to evaluate the charac- teristics of different types of metastases visible by PSMA PET. Also the question has to be addressed whether dynamic im- aging might improve detection of lesions close to the urinary bladder. In addition, we hope that future studies will compare the HBED-CC conjugated PSMA ligand and other promising methods of imaging PC, such as PET imaging with 18F-FACBC [ 14]. Conclusion This study presents a retrospective comparison between the established 18F-fluoromethylcholine-based PET/CT and a novel method of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand in the diagnosis of recurr

source: context/PSMA/Comparison of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand and 18F-choline-based PETCT for the diagnosis of recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: as PET imaging with 18F-FACBC [ 14]. Conclusion This study presents a retrospective comparison between the established 18F-fluoromethylcholine-based PET/CT and a novel method of PET imaging with a 68Ga-labelled PSMA ligand in the diagnosis of recurrent PC. Our experience with 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT strongly suggests that this is an easy to handle method which can detect PC relapses and metastases with significantly improved contrast when compared to choline-based PET/CT. Nevertheless, the most significant advantages of 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT are the sensitive detection of lesions even at low PSA levels, of even small lymph node metastases (primarily due to a high radiotracer uptake) and of central bone and liver metastases due to low background signal. text: Current Status of Prostate-Speciﬁc Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine: Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-SpeciﬁcM e m b r a n e Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer Clemens Kratochwil, MD,* Ali Afshar-Oromieh, MD,*,† Klaus Kopka, PhD,‡ Uwe Haberkorn, MD,*,† and Frederik L. Giesel, MD* The prostate-speciﬁc membrane antigen (PSMA) is expressed by approximately 90% of prostate carcinomas. The expression correlates with unfavorable prognostic factors, such as a high Gleason score, inﬁltrative growth, metastasis, and hormone-independence. The high speciﬁcity, especially in the undifferentiated stage, makes it an excellent target for diagnosis and therapy. Therefore, antibodies and small molecule inhibitors have been developed for imaging and therapy. In 2011 PSMA-11, a ligand that consists of the Glu-urea-motif and the chelator HBED-CC, which can be exclusively radiolabeled with 68Ga for PET imaging, presented the clinical breakthrough for prostate cancer diagnostics. In two large diagnostic studies ( n ¼ 319 and n ¼ 248) PET/CT with PSMA-11 successfully localized the recurrent tumor in approximately 90% of patients with biochemical relapse. Integrating PSMA-PET/CT into the planning phase of radiotherapy, the treatment concept is changed in 30%-50% of the patients. The combination of the Glu-urea-motif with DOTA, which can be labeled with several diagnostic and therapeutic radionuc lides, opened new avenues for therapeutic usage of the small-molecule PSMA ligands. In the beginning of 2016, there are four conﬁrmative reports ( n ¼ 19, n ¼ 24, n ¼ 30, andn ¼ 56) from four different centers reporting a PSA response in approximately 70% of patients treated with177Lu-labeled PSMA ligands. In conclusion, the data available

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: beginning of 2016, there are four conﬁrmative reports ( n ¼ 19, n ¼ 24, n ¼ 30, andn ¼ 56) from four different centers reporting a PSA response in approximately 70% of patients treated with177Lu-labeled PSMA ligands. In conclusion, the data available up to now indicate a widespread use of PSMA ligands for diagnostic applications with respect to staging, detection of recurrence, or metastases in patients with rising tumor markers and for therapy in case of failure of guideline-compliant treatment. Semin Nucl Med 46:405-418 C 2016 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved. http://dx.doi.org/10.1053/j.semnuclmed.2016.04.004 405 0001-2998/& 2016 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved. *Department of Nuclear Medicine, University Hospital Heidelberg, Heidelberg, Germany. †Clinical Cooperation Unit Nuclear Medicine (E060), German Cancer Research Center (dkfz), Heidelberg, Germany. ‡Division of Radiopharmaceutical Chemistry, German Cancer Research Center (dkfz), Heidelberg, Germany. Address reprint requests to Uwe Haberkorn, MD, Department of Nuclear Medicine, University Hospital Heidelberg, INF 400, Heidelberg 69120, Germany. E-mail: uwe.haberkorn@med.uni-heidelberg.de Background About Prostate Cancer: Epidemiology, Current Standard Procedures and the Unsolved Clinical Demand for Improvement T oday, with 240,000 diagnosed men in the United States1 and 420,000 in Europe,2 prostate cancer (PC) is one of the most common malignancies in the world. Despite the good prognosis of indolent PC, even the limited number of aggressive tumors translates into a high lethality, which also presents a tremendous increase over time. In 1990, only 156,000 men died of PC; but in 2010, the number had already increased to 256,000 deaths worldwide.3 Screening and initial diagnosis is traditionally based on serum

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: o a high lethality, which also presents a tremendous increase over time. In 1990, only 156,000 men died of PC; but in 2010, the number had already increased to 256,000 deaths worldwide.3 Screening and initial diagnosis is traditionally based on serum tests for prostate-speciﬁca n t i g e n (PSA) and sonography-guided needle biopsy. However, during the last decade noninvasive multiparametric MRI presented some improvement helping to decide on the indication, type, and localization of a prostatebiopsy for cancer diagnosis.4 The treatment for localized PC is tailored into active surveillance, radical prostatectomy, radiotherapy, or other focal therapy, depending on tumor characteristics, Gleason score, PSA value, and the patient’s life expectancy. Treatment with a curative intent is not used in all patients with localized PC, because many cases of well-to-moderately differentiated PCs have a very indolent course and are not lethal. In contrast to other solid tumors, active surveillance can be the strategy of choice even for potentially curable patients. Thus, for low-risk patients, conven- tional imaging still contributes less to the staging procedures before further treatment stratiﬁcation.5 The clinical challenge for diagnostic imaging of primary tumors presents the differ- entiation between indolent forms of PC and aggressive ones. The latter contribute approximately 15% of the prostate tumors.6 The oncologic standard tracer FDG is not widely used in PC as primary tumors as well as lymph node metastases present a variable glucose dependency, and detection rates for well-to-moderate differentiated tumors have been found low, that is, in the dimension of 30%.7,8 Attempts to use amino acid tracers resulted into similar variable success with signiﬁcant limitations to either

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: ucose dependency, and detection rates for well-to-moderate differentiated tumors have been found low, that is, in the dimension of 30%.7,8 Attempts to use amino acid tracers resulted into similar variable success with signiﬁcant limitations to either sensitivity (eg,11C-methionine) or speciﬁc- ity (eg, 18F-FACBC).5,9 Targeting lipid metabolism with11C- acetate presented more promising data5; but because of the short half-life of11C (20 minutes), this tracer is not suitable for clinical routine.11C-choline, a tracer representing membrane synthesis, demonstrated similar characteristics than11C-acetate but could also beﬂuorinated to receive18F-ﬂuoro-ethyl-choline (FECh) or ﬂuoromethylcholine (FMC). The longer half-life of 18F (110 minutes) was advantageous for clinical routine. Recently, the clinical value of choline-PET/CT was summarized in a meta-analysis.10 Although the pooled detection rate was 62%, the rate for the subgroup reporting dedicated rates for nodal disease and bone metastases were only 36% and 25%, respectively. Thus, the limited detection rate or the sensitivity of PC imaging in the pre-PSMA era were somehow unsatisfying, leading to further research developing new diagnostic targets. The Prostate-Speciﬁc Membrane Antigen Represents an Ideal Target Structure The prostate-speciﬁc membrane antigen (PSMA), also known as folate hydrolase I or glutamate carboxypeptidase II, shows several biological features making it an ideal target structure for ligand development for nuclear medicine procedures. In particular, PSMA is a type II transmembrane protein with glutamate-carboxypeptidase activity and thus, based on known substrates it is possible to develop small-molecule PSMA-targeted radiopharmaceuticals. In contrast to antibod- ies, small molecules show a fast blo

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: is a type II transmembrane protein with glutamate-carboxypeptidase activity and thus, based on known substrates it is possible to develop small-molecule PSMA-targeted radiopharmaceuticals. In contrast to antibod- ies, small molecules show a fast blood clearance leading to a low background activity. Furthermore, after the binding of the ligand to its membrane-anchored target, internalization occurs via clathrin-coated pits and endocytosis, resulting in an effective transportation of the bound molecule into the cells ending up in the late endosomes. 11 This leads to enhanced tumor uptake and retention, and it results in high-image quality for diagnostic procedures and a high local dose for therapeutic applications. As already mentioned, PSMA is a cell- surface protein that shows a signiﬁcant overexpression on most prostatic cancer cells, especially in advanced-stage prostate carcinomas and no expression or only a low expression in normal human tissues. 12,13 Several studies revealed an associ- ation of PSMA with stage and grade, showing increasing PSMA levels with higher stages and grading of the tumor.13-15 Owing to the fact that PSMA is expressed in most prostate carcinomas, many attempts dealt with the development of potential binders to this enzyme. Besides antibodies, 16,17 targeted nanoparticles18,19 and aptamer conjugates,20,21 other strategies used small-moleculeinhibitors developed by indus- try for their modi ﬁcation to radiopharmaceuticals.22-24 The ﬁrst antibody used was the monoclonal antibody 7E11-C5 that was approved by the Food and Drug Administartion as ProstaScint. 25 However, 7E11-C5 was found to bind to the intracellular part of PSMA, preventing its binding to intact cells and therefore, leading to very-low detection rates. 26 In con- trast, the secon

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: proved by the Food and Drug Administartion as ProstaScint. 25 However, 7E11-C5 was found to bind to the intracellular part of PSMA, preventing its binding to intact cells and therefore, leading to very-low detection rates. 26 In con- trast, the second generation antibody J591 binds to the extracellular domain of PSMA and has been used in clinical trials showing improved targeting of PC. 27 Although antibodies offer potential for tumor targeting, their effectiveness as diagnostic radiopharmaceuticals is limited by a long biological half-life and poor tumor penetrability. Approaches that may overcome these limita- tions are combining antibodies with the longer-lived PET radionuclides 89Zr and 64Cu28 or using single-chain frag- ments or the anti-PSMA minibody89Zr-Df-IAB2M as smaller variants of the humanized J591. Studies on the development of small-molecule inhibitors mimicking the endogenous substrate N-acetyl-L-aspartyl-L- glutamate (NAAG), which is cleaved by N-acetylated alpha- linked acidic dipeptidase NAALADase or glutamate carboxy- peptidase II, identi ﬁed various candidates. 29,30 The insight into the structural and functional 31,32 homology between NAALADase and PSMA paved the way to the use of these small molecules in targeted treatment and imaging of PC. C. Kratochwil et al.406 Following this line, several small-molecule PSMA inhibitors labeled with a variety of radionuclides were developed based on the structural motifs of various NAALADase inhibitors. 33-41 In 2008, the ﬁrst diagnostic high-af ﬁnity small-molecule inhibitors of PSMA, 123I-MIP-1072 and 123I-MIP-1095 were introduced into the clinic. In men with metastatic PC, SPECT/CT using these molecules demon- strated the ability to rapidly detect lesions in soft tissue, bone, and the prostate gland as early

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: inhibitors of PSMA, 123I-MIP-1072 and 123I-MIP-1095 were introduced into the clinic. In men with metastatic PC, SPECT/CT using these molecules demon- strated the ability to rapidly detect lesions in soft tissue, bone, and the prostate gland as early as 1-4 hours after injection. 38 PSMA-PET/CT for Primary Staging To correctly diagnose the primary tumor of PC, can be challenging. Approximately 70% of all PCs are located in the peripheral zone, and there is no obstacle to identify and biopsy most of these tumors with transrectal ultrasonography. How- ever, some tumors are located in the anterior peripheral zone ventral of the urethra and also 20% of tumors are located in the central and 10% tumors in the transition zone. This particular subgroup of primary PC is challenging to be localized and often need repeated biopsies until the ﬁnal diagnoses can be done. 42 In addition, approximately 85% of PCs are multi- focal.43 As a consequence, Gleason score derived from biopsy and succeeding prostatectomy only matched in 69% of the patients evaluated in a comparative trial. 44 As active surveil- lance becomes more and more important in elderly popula- tions, the effect of risk stratiﬁcation for patient tailoring, which is largely based on the Gleason score increases the clinical effect to ﬁnd the dominant intraprostatic lesion. Choline-PET had high detection rates for well-to-moderate differentiated PC but sensitivity decreases with dedifferentiation. 10 In contrast, FDG- PET was only found valuable to identify highly aggressive tumors but presents false-negative in low-and-intermediate Gleason scores. 8 Thus, this clinical need is still unsolved. The literature for PSMA-PET/CT regarding initial PC diagnosis is still rare. In a small study, presurgery PSMA-PET/CT was compared t

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: ors but presents false-negative in low-and-intermediate Gleason scores. 8 Thus, this clinical need is still unsolved. The literature for PSMA-PET/CT regarding initial PC diagnosis is still rare. In a small study, presurgery PSMA-PET/CT was compared to extensive histologic workup of 112 segments evaluated after six prostatectomies. Despite the physiological uptake in normal prostate tissue, which causes some back- ground accumulation, true positive tumors presented with signiﬁcant higher standardized uptake values (SUVs) resulting in positive and negative predictive values of 96% and 85%, respectively. The SUVs of 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT correlated also weakly with Gleason score, but due to limited patient number no testing for signiﬁcance was reasonable.45 Another group compared PSMA-PET/CT with multiparametric MRI (Fig. 1) in a blind read using the PI-RADS standard reporting schema.46 If MRI was considered as reference, PSMA-PET/CT presented 90% concordantﬁndings, whereas 97% of lesions detected by PSMA-PET/CT presented a concordantﬁnding in MRI. In a clinical phase-2 trial, the99mTc-labeled PSMA ligand MIP-1404 presented sensitivity and speciﬁcity of 94% and 100%, respectively, for detecting primary PC in a preselected high-risk population47 and now its clinical effect (in conse- quence, patients are tailored to active surveillance vs radical prostatectomy) is evaluated in a prospective phase-3 clinical trial (NCT02615067). The PSMA-targeted imaging thus seems highly promising to improve imaging-guided biopsy by reducing false-negative biopsies and guiding the needle to the lesion with the highest Gleason score, bringing minimal- invasive needle biopsy closer to the level of full prostate histology, strengthening its reliability for patient stratiﬁcation. Imaging-Guided R

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: -negative biopsies and guiding the needle to the lesion with the highest Gleason score, bringing minimal- invasive needle biopsy closer to the level of full prostate histology, strengthening its reliability for patient stratiﬁcation. Imaging-Guided Radiotherapy and Surgery A second challenge in the patient management is the decision between primary radiotherapy and radical prostatectomy either with or without extended pelvic lymphadenectomy or radiation of the lymphatic drainage. Several classi ﬁcation criteria have been proposed to optimize patient management, for example, the D’Amico classiﬁcation that is based on the PSA level, Gleason score, and T staging. 48 In addition, formulae based on epidemiologic data have been published to prether- apeutically estimate the risk f or nodal-positive disease.49,50 There are some controversies about the best risk-stratiﬁcation system as well as about deriving the correct clinical conse- quence. If the risk for N þ exceeds 420%, it is common to include the pelvic lymph drainage into the radiation volume. However, this concept translates into undertreatment of up to 20% low-risk patients and overtreatment in up to 80% of high- risk patients, making more targeted approaches desirable. In a recent publication, 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT as well as conven- tional imaging-staging procedures (including bone scan, CT, and MRI) were performed with 57 patients scheduled to primary radiotherapy. In 50.8% of the cases, therapy was changed (Fig. 2). 51,52 These data could be con ﬁrmed by a different group that found an overall change in decision- making in 53.7% of their 44 patients.53 In the setting of salvage radiation treatment, a third group found a major management change in 29% of patients after enhancing conventional staging with PSMA-PET/CT

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: up that found an overall change in decision- making in 53.7% of their 44 patients.53 In the setting of salvage radiation treatment, a third group found a major management change in 29% of patients after enhancing conventional staging with PSMA-PET/CT. In 9% of the patients, they found metastatic lesions outside the prostatic fossa and pelvic lymph nodes, that is, a change from cN1-cM1. 54 With regard to surgery, the nomenclature for limited, standard, and extended pelvic lymph node dissection (PLND) lacks standardization that makes it difﬁcult to compare differ- ent reports. Although limited PLND is considered a diagnostic procedure that can be performed during prostatectomy with- out additional time consumption and low complications, the standard and extended PLND were found to correlate with patient prognoses according to the large SEER dataset. 54 However, only 25% of the lymph node metastases are in the area of standard PLND predominantly around the internal iliac artery.54 The extended PLND (including the area between the ureters) is time consuming and can cause additional and more severe complications, for example, injury to the presacral plexus. The use of sentinel lymph node (SLN) marker techniques revealed that several patients present with untypical lymphatic drainage, for example, pararectal to the mesenteric or into inguinal lymph nodes; these regions are even outside the extended PLND area. 55 Nevertheless, lymph node meta- stases to the SLN region only were reported in only 41% of Current status of PSMA targeting in nuclear medicine 407 patients56 and therefore the SLN concept is less valuable in prostate than in breast cancer or melanoma. The current radiological standard, the evaluation of short-axis diameter of pelvic lymph nodes, presented relevant lim

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: ng in nuclear medicine 407 patients56 and therefore the SLN concept is less valuable in prostate than in breast cancer or melanoma. The current radiological standard, the evaluation of short-axis diameter of pelvic lymph nodes, presented relevant limitations, 57 which is unsatisfactory for surgeons to stratify their patients. Thus, new techniques for tailoring patients regarding the extent of lymph node dissection are still of signiﬁcant clinical interest. A recent study evaluating 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT found 49 positive lymph nodes including 38 (78%) with a diameter ofo8m m (Figs. 3and 4). The authors came to the conclusion that PSMA- PET/CT is more sensitive than CT-based 3-dimensional volumetric lymph node evaluation in determining the node status of patients with recurrent PC. 58 Despite the low patient number, these results have now been reproduced by a second center59 and in the setting of primary tumor staging a thorough histologic validation has been performed.60 However, there is still no proof that improved diagnostics would translate into improved survival. Nevertheless, preliminary results are prom- ising. Extended lymph node dissection, targeted with the use of presurgery PSMA-PET/CT imaging, was performed to 17 oligometastatic patients and PSA stayed less than 0.2 ng/mL for 14 patients despite the fact that no postsurgery therapy was performed in 8 of them.61 Thus, further studies in theﬁeld of presurgery PSMA imaging are needed for a possible inclusion into PC guidelines. An additional idea is to use a hand-held gamma probes for detection of111In-labeled PSMA ligands in dedicated lesions during surgery. Currently, only a case report for combining presurgery68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT and an on- site gamma-probe measurement of111In-PSMA-I&T is avail- able.62 However

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: gamma probes for detection of111In-labeled PSMA ligands in dedicated lesions during surgery. Currently, only a case report for combining presurgery68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT and an on- site gamma-probe measurement of111In-PSMA-I&T is avail- able.62 However, the preliminary results reported by this group already seem very promising and the lower kidney, spleen, and soft tissue uptake seen 24 hours p.i. with the novel ligand PSMA-617 in comparison with the earlier ligands like PSMA- I&T 62 and PSMA-11 ( Fig. 5) might further improve lesion delineation during surgery. PSMA Imaging of Recurrent PC The most extensive data about the clinical effect of PSMA-PET/ CT are available in the setting of biochemical recurrence (BCR). Figure 1 A 61-year-old patient with a biopsy-proven prostate cancer in the left peripheral zone, presented in multiparametric MRI with diffusion restriction in DWI (A), contrast media uptake in ceT1w (B), and hyperintensive in T2w (D). Identical location and a near-total correlation of tumor extend was also demonstrated in 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT (C, red arrow). PSMA-PET also diagnoseda locoregional lymph node metastasis (C, white arrow) that was retrospectively also delineable in DWI (A) and ceT1w (B). ceT1w, contrast-enhanced T1-weighted image; DWI, diffusion-weighted imaging; T2w, T2-weighted image. C. Kratochwil et al.408 Afshar-Oromieh et al63 retrospectively analyzed the diagnostic value of 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT in a large cohort of 319 BCR patients with histologic validation in a subgroup of them. A lesion-based analysis demonstrated sensitivity, speci ﬁcity, negative predictive value, an d positive predictive value of 76.6%, 100%, 91.4%, and 100%, respectively; on a patient- based analysis, the sensitivity was 88.1%. Tumor detection was positively associated

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: alysis demonstrated sensitivity, speci ﬁcity, negative predictive value, an d positive predictive value of 76.6%, 100%, 91.4%, and 100%, respectively; on a patient- based analysis, the sensitivity was 88.1%. Tumor detection was positively associated with PSA level but not with PSA doubling time (PSAdt). Eiber et al64 analyzed 248 patients and, highly similar to the 88.1% of Afshar-Oromieh et al, found pathologic scans in 89.5%. In a conﬁrmative manner, PSAdt also did not inﬂuence the detection rate. The effects of PSA level and PSA kinetics on the detection rate of PSMA-PET/CT have also been investigated in two other studies.65,66 Ceci et al 65 evaluated 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT in 39 patients with PSAo 2n g / m L and PSMA-PET/CT was positive in 59% of them, which is in near-total agreement with the study by Afshar-Oromieh who reported a detection rate of 61% in the 90 patients with PSA o 2 ng/mL. However, in contrast to these two studies they found also a strong dependency from PSAdt. Among the patients with PSA o 2 ng/mL, the detection rate was 85% if the PSAdt was o6.5 months, but only 19% if the PSAdt was 46.5 months. The receiver operating characteristic analysis of Ceci et al also suggested a PSA of 0.83 ng/mL as a cutoff to optimize the diagnostic accuracy of PSMA-PET/CT. Eiber et al, 64 Morigi et al,66 and an earlier work of Afshar- Oromieh et al67 also provided subgroup analysis for patients with PSA values between 0.2 and 0.5 ng/mL. The detection rates were in good accordance with 57.9%, 50%, and 50%, respectively. This is an interesting collective. However, in this single case during GRPr-PET the PSA was 6.8 ng/mL that had already increased to 9.2 ng/mL when the PSMA-PET was performed. 72 Therefore, even in the PSMA era, GRPr targeting remains of relevant interest,especially as the two compounds have distinct physiological biodistribution that might be of relevance regarding the dose-limiting organs if the tracers are used during radionuclide therapy. The diagnostic relevance of addressing two different target structures regarding the intra- individual and interindividual tumor heterogeneity needs further evaluation. Recently, a novel bispeci ﬁcP S M A / G R P r targeting radioligand was evaluated preclinically and demon- strated similar receptor af ﬁnities than its respective mono- mer. 73 However, it should be noted that these bispeci ﬁc ligands have to be carefully designed and optimized in pharmacokinetics as they may increase the background activity by accumulation in a broader range of normal organs. Therefore, conventional bone scan remains the mainstay for follow-up imaging of metastasized castration-resistant PC (mCRPC) in clinical routine, despite a higher sensitivity and speciﬁcity of 18F-ﬂuoride PET/CT has already been demonstrated.74 On the contrary, reaching the metastatic stage brings in new challenges in the management of patients with PC. As all effective treatments are associated with clinical side effects and also “economical toxicities”are of particular interest to either ﬁnd predictive stratiﬁcation criteria for a particular treatment or to conduct an early evaluation of response to stop ineffective treatments soon. Regarding the androgen-deprivation therapy, 18F-ﬂuoro-dihydrotestosterone was used to image androgen receptors (ARs) and demonstrated feasible to document the competitive binding of the drug ﬂutamide. 75 However, 18F- ﬂuoro-dihydrotestosterone is a very speciﬁc method, whereas androgen-deprivation therapy is normally well tolerated and also quite cheap; therefore, the clinic

In conventional bone scan or CT, it is possible that previously occult osteolytic lesions become sclerotic after successful treatment and their appearance on the ﬁrst follow-up examination canbe misinterpreted as progres- sion (Fig. 7); this well-known “ﬂare-phenomenon” prohibits early response assessment during the ﬁrst 12 weeks after changing the treatment regime. However, the“ﬂare phenom- enon” is also of clinical signiﬁcance regarding the PSA serum level, limiting its value for response evaluation in a similar manner. 76 In addition, hormones and taxanes have also been reported to downregulate the secretion of PSA per viable tumor cell, which may account for the signi ﬁcantly higher PSA response rates (45%-50%) relative to measurable response rates (8%-17%) reported in docetaxel trials and makes PSA a less suitable surrogate for response quanti ﬁcation during chemotherapy. In contrast, docetaxel was not found to change the expression of PSMA per tumor cell.77 A more controversial discussion is needed regarding primary and secondary hor- mone manipulations. Actually, only one case report is available reporting sequential PSMA-PET/CT for evaluation of response to 223Ra therapy and described a fulminantﬂare-up of 68Ga-PSMA-11 accumulation.84 In con- clusion, the clinical effect of PSMA-PET/CT regarding imaging- based response evaluation has not sufﬁciently been studied, yet. For patient strati ﬁcation with regard to PSMA-targeted radioligand therapy (PSMA-RLT), it is consensus that valida- tion of PSMA-positive tumor phenotype either by PSMA-PET/ CT or scintigraphy is a precondition to receive therapy (Fig. 8). The currently still unsolved question is whether the tumor uptake 1-4 hours p.i., measurable with 68Ga, 18F, or 99mTc containing diagnostics can serve as a predictor for treatment response because the maximum tumor uptake of 177Lu-PSMA- 617 was not found before 20-44 hours p.i. during dosimetry.85 Thus, until now no cutoff was proposed on how patients with a visually week tracer uptake might reasonable be tailored for or against PSMA-RLT (Fig. 9A). PSMA-Targeted Radionuclide Therapy of CRPC Metastasized PC is challenging to treat. Standard ﬁrst-line therapy is conducted with luteinizing hormone –releasing hormone (LHRH) analogues and LHRH antagonists to lower the serum testosterone to castration level. Alternatively, anti- androgens such as bicalutamide are used to competitively block ARs. This primary antiandrogen treatment has a mean duration of 2 years, which can be lower in tumors with high Gleason score. Recently, abiraterone was approved, a drug that inhibits androgen synthesis both in testes and also in the adrenal glands and therefore has a similar but stronger mechanism of action as LHRH analogues. Another novel drug is enzalutamide, an antiandrogen with much higher afﬁnity to the AR than its ancestors. 86 After primary and secondary hormone manipulations have failed, the mCRPC presents with an inherent resistance against most conventional chemothera- pies, with taxanes being the only exception. However, it has been reported that tubulin targeting with taxanes inhibits AR nuclear translocation. 87 As abiraterone or enzalutamide also interfere with AR signaling, these drugs are somehow com- petitive in their mechanism of action and cross-resistance may occur, making optimal sequencing of the new drug

Current status of PSMA targeting in nuclear medicine 413 localized PC and for palliative management of mCRPC, and even radiopharmaceuticals targeting the surrounding bone matrix instead of the tumor itself can improve survival. 89 Therefore, it seemed more promising that PSMA ligands that target tumor cells directly and which are internalized into the cells will be effective in deliv ering high doses for systemic PSMA-RLT. In contrast to conventional pharmaceuticals, the toxicity and response proba bility of a radiopharmaceutical predominantly depends on the radiation-absorbed dose to healthy and tumor tissue, respectively. There are well-deﬁned radiation tolerance limits for normal organs. Therefore, empiri- cal dose escalation studies can partially be omitted and dosing of radioactive drugs can be based on dosimetry. The radiolabeled antibody 177Lu-J591 alr

text: pectively. There are well-deﬁned radiation tolerance limits for normal organs. Therefore, empiri- cal dose escalation studies can partially be omitted and dosing of radioactive drugs can be based on dosimetry. The radiolabeled antibody 177Lu-J591 already demon- strated moderate antitumor effects, but the slow diffusion of antibodies into solid lesions with an uptake of max- imum 6-7 days p.i. and hematotoxicity caused by a long circulation time in blood (red marrow dose ¼ 0.3 Gy/ GBq) were limitations. 90 Owing to faster kinetics, the PSMA-targeted small-molecule 131I-MIP-1095 demon- strated a superior therapeutic range in comparison to the antibody approach with PSA responses in 17 of 28 patients who were treated with a single cycle of mean 4.8 GBq. 91 However, the quantitatively high co-emission of high-energy photons arising from 131I-MIP1095 in the whole-body remainder contr ibuted relevantly to the red marrow dose (0.3 Gy/GBq) and it also requires extensive radiation protection efforts. 91 Unlike 131I, 177Lu has a better beta-to-gamma ratio that makes it preferable for clinical routine and reduces whole-body dose. Therefore, PSMA ligands carrying more universal chelates were developed. 177Lu-DOTAGA-(I-y)fk(Sub-KuE) was administered to a larger patient series ( n ¼ 56).92 High- absorbed tumor doses of median 3.3 Gy/GBq compared to normal organs of 0.8 Gy/GBq for kidneys or 1.3 Gy/GBq for salivary glands as well as a high rate of overall PSA responders without presence of severe acute toxicity were reported. The main limitation was a high variability of treatment activity (range: 3.6-8.7 GBq), which makes a comparison of these results to the ﬁndings of other studies difﬁcult. PSMA-617 consists of t h eg l u - u r e am o t i fa n dt h e DOTA chelator separated by a lipophilic-linker region, which favorable interacts with the hydrophobic accessory pocket that was found next to the catalytic domain of PSMA by Baiinka et al. 93 DOTA can be labeled with positron emitters like 44Sc or 68Ga, gamma emitters like 111In, beta particle emitters like 90Yo r 177Lu or alpha particle emitters like 213Bi or 225Ac. Tumor doses between 1.2 and 47.5 Gy/GBq (mean ¼ 13.1 Gy/GBq) have been estimated by the Munich 97 or between 6 and 22 Gy/GBq by the Heidelberg 85 group. One of the earliest patients treated with two cycles of 177Lu-PSMA-617 with a cumula tive activity of 7.4 GBq presented a radiological complete response. 99 Succeeding case reports con ﬁrmatively presented patients with marked response to PSMA-RLT, even in challenging situations. 100,101 More structured data have been reported recently. In a case series of 24 patients receiving two cycles of 177Lu-PSMA-617 with a mean dose of 6 GBq per cycle, the PSA response rate after the ﬁrst cycle was approx- imately 80%. However, some patients presented with early relapse and in only 70% of the patients, the PSA response was sustained until 8-10 weeks after cycle-1. 102 The other case series reported 30 patients, receiving up to three cycles of 177Lu-PSMA-617 therapy bimonthly with a typical treatment activity of 6 GBq. 85 Conﬁrmatively, they found PSA response (any decline of PSA at week-8) in 70% of the patients after cycle-1. Again, the response was of limited duration in a portion of patients and only approximately 40% of the patients intended-to-treat presented with ongoing response until the restaging was p e r f o r m e di nw e e k - 2 4 ,t h a ti s ,6m o n t h sa n d3c y c l e sa f t e r initiation of treatment ( Fig. 9 ). Beside few patients who di

text: imately 40% of the patients intended-to-treat presented with ongoing response until the restaging was p e r f o r m e di nw e e k - 2 4 ,t h a ti s ,6m o n t h sa n d3c y c l e sa f t e r initiation of treatment ( Fig. 9 ). Beside few patients who died early after therapy by tumor progression, both centers only reported grade-1/2 hematologic toxicities and sporadically mild xerostomia and fatigue as side effects. According to the recommendations of the PC clinical trials ’ working-group criteria, both centers explic- itly reported 450% PSA decline rate at week-8, which was found in good accordance with 41.6 % 102 and 43.3%85 for 6 GBq 177Lu-PSMA-617, respectively. In contrast, a smaller case series was treated with a higher activity of 7.4 GBq tagged to the ligand 177Lu-PSMA-I&T a n dr e p o r t e da450% PSA response rate of 33% at week- 8.103 Regardless of the 177Lu-labeled PSMA ligand used, all four authors reporting case series con ﬁrmatively reported well tolerability of PSMA-targeted RLT with no or only few grade-3/4 toxicities. 85,92,102,103 Nevertheless, all authors reported a portion of patients (20%-40%) that were refractory to treatment with 177Lu-PSMA ligands despite a suf ﬁcient expression of PSMA in the preceding PSMA-PET/CT. Patients with diffuse-type red marrow inﬁltration that was considered a risk factor to develop higher hematotoxicity, 85 present another challenge. Replacing the beta particle emitter 177Lu with an alpha particle emitter such as 213Bi or 225Ac is one promising option to overcome these limitations. 104 Preliminary results ( Fig. 10 ) are more than promising. 105 Conclusion and Future Perspectives on PSMA Imaging As outlined above, there are seve ral interesting application ﬁelds on how PSMA targeting in Nuclear Medicine can improve the

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: ations. 104 Preliminary results ( Fig. 10 ) are more than promising. 105 Conclusion and Future Perspectives on PSMA Imaging As outlined above, there are seve ral interesting application ﬁelds on how PSMA targeting in Nuclear Medicine can improve the clinical management of patients with PC. The already available experience provides a quite clear preview about the possibilities of these new techniques. However, some aspects need further validation with higher patient numbers or inside a prospective clinical trial. Despite ﬁrst being described as a quite prostate-speci ﬁc target, PSMA has also been demonstrated in the neovasculature of several other kinds of cancer, such as kidney, bladder, lung, Figure 10 Maximum intensity projections (MIP) of68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CTs. Initially, a patient with bulky lymph node metastases and inﬁltration of the liver presented with PSA of294 ng/mL (A). After two cycles of 7.4 GBq177Lu-PSMA-617 therapies, imaging demonstrated progression of the pelvic and abdominal lesions and PSA increased to 419 ng/mL (B). After two additional cycles now conducted with 6.5 MBq 225Ac-PSMA-617, the next restagingrevealed favorable response and accordingly the PSA dropped to 3.5 ng/mL (C). Current status of PSMA targeting in nuclear medicine 415 breast, colorectal, and pancreatic cancers, and in melanoma.106 However, only few preliminary case reports but no systematic evaluation about the diagnostic accuracy of PSMA-PET/CT for non–prostate tumors is currently available, yet. Further studies are also needed to evaluate if the PSMA expressed in the neovasculature of these tumors internalizes after binding of a radioactive ligand, this would be an additional prerequisite for the therapeutic application of PSMA-RLT against other malignancies. Recently, theﬁrst 68Ge/

source: context/PSMA/Current Status of Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Targeting in Nuclear Medicine - Clinical Translation of Chelator Containing Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Ligands Into Diagnostics and Therapy for Prostate Cancer.pdf
text: e if the PSMA expressed in the neovasculature of these tumors internalizes after binding of a radioactive ligand, this would be an additional prerequisite for the therapeutic application of PSMA-RLT against other malignancies. Recently, theﬁrst 68Ge/68Ga generator system was approved by the European Medicines Agency and a single-vial kit solution for radiolabeling 68Ga-PSMA-11 at room temperature has been developed.107 Thus, 68Ga-PSMA-11 should become similarly practicable for clinical routine like using a conven- tional 99mTc tracer kit or like18F-PSMA ligands provided with a satellite concept.
