Discussion The aim of our retrospective analysis was to present a more accurate evaluation of the performance of [ 68Ga]Ga-PSMA- 11 PET/CT in a large multicentre patient cohort with recurrent PC after prostatectomy by considering recent knowledge about PSMA imaging. Only patients with recurrent PC after prostatectomy were included in this largest cohort yet analysed. Among the patients included in this analysis, 68.9% pre- sented with at least one lesion characteristic for PC in [ 68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT. This rate is lower compared with the second-largest and third-largest patient cohort includ- ing 1007 and 635 patients with overall PET positivity rates of 79.5 and 75%, respectively [4, 7]. This difference finds poten- tial explanation in the lower median PSA of our cohort (0.8 ng/ml) compared with the two before-mentioned publica- tions (2.2 ng/ml and 2.1 ng/ml, respectively). The PET positivity rate was significantly associated with the PSA value beginning at 43% for PSA < 0.2 ng/ml and rising to 93% at PSA > 10 ng/ml (Fig.1). A direct comparison of each PSA subgroup to previously published data is chal- lenging because of different subgroup definitions. However, for PSA < 0.2 ng/ml, PET positivity rates of 46 –47% have been reported [ 2, 4]. These rates are higher when compared with our analysis (43%). For PSA values up to 0.5 ng/ml, 38– 58% PET positivity rates have been previously observed [ 3, 7]. These differences find explanation in the patient selection. As mentioned before, our exclusion criteria were stricter com- pared with previous studies thereby being clos

source: context/PSMA/Performance of [68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET-CT in patients with recurrent prostate cancer after prostatectomy-a multi-centre evaluation of 2533 patients.pdf
text: 0.5 ng/ml, 38– 58% PET positivity rates have been previously observed [ 3, 7]. These differences find explanation in the patient selection. As mentioned before, our exclusion criteria were stricter com- pared with previous studies thereby being closer to the “true rate” for this type of patients. In addition, we note that our data present with closer confidence intervals in the PSA sub- groups (Fig. 1 and Table 2) compared with previous data [ 4], suggesting more precise estimations of the cor- responding positivity rates. 2928 Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2021) 48:2925–2934 As previously reported in different studies, also in our analysis, the rate of pathologic scans did not reach 100% because a proportion of PC does not sufficiently express PSMA. In some other cases, tumour lesions could poten- tially be obscured by excretion of [ 68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 via the urinary tract. Attention should be drawn to the fact that all PET positivity rates presented by this evaluation are only valid for scans conducted at 1 h p.i.. It is known from several publications including different PSMA ligands that scans at time points later than 1 h p.i. (e.g. 1.5 h or 3 h p.i.) show the majority of PC lesions with higher tracer uptake and contrast [ 1, 11–15], therefore resulting in higher numbers of detected PC lesions as well as a higher number of patients with a pathologic scan [16, 17]. Despite these data in favour of late scans, [ 68Ga]Ga- PSMA-11 PET/CT is routinely conducted at 1 h p.i. in the majority of centres, probably because of its first described clinical setup [1]. The multivariable analysis also demonstrated a signif- icant association between a pathologic [ 68Ga]Ga-PSMA- 11 PET/CT and higher GSC. In previous studies includ- ing bigger cohorts of patients with available GSC (n =

source: context/PSMA/Performance of [68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET-CT in patients with recurrent prostate cancer after prostatectomy-a multi-centre evaluation of 2533 patients.pdf
text: t described clinical setup [1]. The multivariable analysis also demonstrated a signif- icant association between a pathologic [ 68Ga]Ga-PSMA- 11 PET/CT and higher GSC. In previous studies includ- ing bigger cohorts of patients with available GSC (n = 221–864), the association between GSC and a higher probability of a pathologic PET/CT was controversial [2–4]. With 2112 patients with available GSC informa- tion included in the current analysis (Table 1), we as- sume that our calculation is sufficiently powered. Also here, we note that our data present with closer confi- dence intervals in the GSC subgroups (Fig. 2 and Table 2) compared with previous data [4 ], suggesting more precise estimations of t he corresponding positivity rates. The positive impact of higher GSC on PSMA-PET/ Fig. 1 Probability of a pathologic [68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT as histogram and plot of the rates of pathologic scans with confidence intervals depending on PSA levels in 2407 patients. As shown by the figure, the probability of a pathologic scan rose with higher PSA values. The multivariable analysis demonstrated a signifi- cant association between a patho- logic scan and higher PSA 2929Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2021) 48:2925–2934 CT is supported by preclinical studies in which a posi- tive correlation between higher GSC and PSMA expres- sion has been reported [ 18–20]. In accordance with previous publications including patient cohorts > n = 200 [ 2–4], we did not find an association be- tween a pathologic scan and age or injected tracer doses. However, we note that the common doses of injected [ 68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 (2 –3 MBq/Kg body weight) are suffi- cient for imaging at 1 h p.i. With regard to the above- mentioned observation that later imaging seems superior, higher injected doses than 2 –3M B q

text: er, we note that the common doses of injected [ 68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 (2 –3 MBq/Kg body weight) are suffi- cient for imaging at 1 h p.i. With regard to the above- mentioned observation that later imaging seems superior, higher injected doses than 2 –3M B q / K gb o d yw e i g h ts e e m consequent. In contrast to some studies including smaller patient co- horts [3, 8, 21, 22], our analyses showed no association be- tween higher probabilities of a pathologic scan and PSA DT or PSAVel, though the sample size for this analysis was still limited. Considering our data, a correlation between PSMA expression and tumour proliferation seems very unlikely. In fact, also our clinical experiences suggest that the contrast of PC lesions only depends on their PSMA expression: lesions of similar sizes and clinical constellations often present with dif- ferent contrast —depending on their PSMA expression as shown by Fig. 3. We therefore assume that the statistical as- sessments of the mentioned studies including smaller patient cohorts were underpowered. Beside the advantages of our current evaluation, namely, the more strict inclusion criteria, the huge cohort, the narrower confidence intervals and the multicentre character, we note several limitations of our study including the heterogeneity of imaging parameters such as diuretics administration. Recently, publications showed that the systematic administra- tion of diuretics and hydration can help to increase tumour Fig. 2 Probability of a pathologic [68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT as histogram and plot of the rates of pathologic scans with confidence intervals depending on GSC in 2112 patients. As shown by the figure, the probability of a patho- logic scan rose with higher GSC, therefore with higher aggressive- ness of the tumour. The multivar- i

source: context/PSMA/Performance of [68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET-CT in patients with recurrent prostate cancer after prostatectomy-a multi-centre evaluation of 2533 patients.pdf
text: t of the rates of pathologic scans with confidence intervals depending on GSC in 2112 patients. As shown by the figure, the probability of a patho- logic scan rose with higher GSC, therefore with higher aggressive- ness of the tumour. The multivar- iable analysis demonstrated a sig- nificant association between a pathologic scan and higher GSC 2930 Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2021) 48:2925–2934 visibility [16, 23]. However, an early administration shortly after tracer injection could wash out the tracer before it had time to bind to PSMA, thereby reducing tumour visibility as well as image quality [ 23]. The optimal time of diuretics ad- ministration has yet to be defined. Our cohort also presents with many missing individual clinical data. For instance, we could not include an additional analysis of patients who had exclusively prostatectomy vs. those who had prostatectomy plus additional radiation therapy because data about the type of the radiation therapy (prostatic bed and/or lymphatic tract and/or concomitant temporary ADT) were insufficient. Since 2018, it is known that ADT can make the results of PSMA imaging unpredictable. We therefore excluded patients who were treated with ADT within the last 6 months before the PET. Given the lack of evidence, it remains unclear wheth- er 6 months free of ADT is sufficiently long for restoring the unaffected status of PSMA expression. More limitations of our analysis are the lack of central standardized reading as well as the missing follow-up of pa- tients in order to estimate the lesion-based negative and pos- itive predictive values, commonly also referred to “specifici- ty” of the methodology. Therefore, the access to reliable follow-up data was limited. However, with regard to biopsy- or histology- proven lesions, interpretation of retrospective data needs to be conducted with great caution because no standardized ap- proach was followed: usually, mainly equivocal findings are further analysed by biopsy, thereby usually producing a bias. In addition, patients with multiple lymph nodes removed by surgery can also produce bias due to individual large number of tumour-affected lymph nodes. On the other hand, the ex- cellent positive predictive value of [ 68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 for recurrent PC has been demonstrated in a multitude of studies [2, 3, 7, 24–28]. According to these publications and our own experiences, any uptake of [ 68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 above local background in morphologically visible lesions is highly spe- cific for PC. Although PSMA ligand uptake has been

source: context/PSMA/Performance of [68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET-CT in patients with recurrent prostate cancer after prostatectomy-a multi-centre evaluation of 2533 patients.pdf
text: ltitude of studies [2, 3, 7, 24–28]. According to these publications and our own experiences, any uptake of [ 68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 above local background in morphologically visible lesions is highly spe- cific for PC. Although PSMA ligand uptake has been reported for various benign and malignant tissues other than PC, their numbers are extremely low compared with those of PC lesions detected everyday by [ 68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT. Conclusion In this multicentre analysis including the largest cohort yet analysed, [ 68Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT at 1 h p.i. confirmed its overall high performance for the detection of recurrent PC. source: context/PSMA/Combined 68 GaGa-PSMA-11 and low-dose 2-18FFDG PETCT using a long-axial field of view scanner for patients referred for 177Lu-PSMA-radioligand therapy.pdf
text: Vol.:(0123456789)1 3 https://doi.or g/10.1007/s00259-022-05961-z SHORT COMMUNICATION Combined [68 Ga]Ga‑PSMA‑11 and low‑dose 2‑[18F]FDG PET/ CT using a long‑axial field of view scanner for patients referred for [177Lu]‑PSMA‑radioligand therapy Ian Alberts1 · Robin Schepers1 · Konstantinos Zeimpekis1 · Hasan Sari1,2 · Axel Rominger1 · Ali Afshar‑Oromieh1 Received: 28 July 2022 / Accepted: 1 September 2022 © The Author(s) 2022 Abstract Purpose Perf orming 2-[ 18F]FDG PET/CT in addition to a PSMA-ligand PET/CT can assist in the detection of lesions with low PSMA expression and may help in prognostication and identification of patients who likely benefit from PSMA- radioligand therapy (PSMA-RLT). However, the cost and time needed for a separate PET/CT examination might hinder its routine implementation. In this communication, we present our initial experiences with additional low-dose 2-[ 18F]FDG PET/CT as part of a dual-tracer and same-day imaging protocol which exploits the higher sensitivity exhibited by long-axial field-of-view (LAFOV) and total-body PET/CT systems and demonstrates its feasibility. Methods Fourteen patients r eferred for evaluation for PSMA-RLT received [68 Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT at 1 h p.i. with a standard activity of 150 MBq and an additional low-dose 2-[18F]FDG PET/CT with 40 MBq 1 h thereafter using a long-axial field-of-view PET/CT system in a single sitting and as per institutional protocol. Scans were scrutinized by two experienced nuclear medicine physicians for mismatch findings. Results The combined prot ocol identified additional lesions with low or absent PSMA-expression but high FDG-avidity in 1/14 (7%) patients. The protocol was easily implemented and well tolerated by all patients. Conclusion Addit ional low-dose 2-[18F]FDG-PET/CT is feasib

source: context/PSMA/Combined 68 GaGa-PSMA-11 and low-dose 2-18FFDG PETCT using a long-axial field of view scanner for patients referred for 177Lu-PSMA-radioligand therapy.pdf
text: prot ocol identified additional lesions with low or absent PSMA-expression but high FDG-avidity in 1/14 (7%) patients. The protocol was easily implemented and well tolerated by all patients. Conclusion Addit ional low-dose 2-[18F]FDG-PET/CT is feasible as part of a same-day imaging protocol and can help reveal lesions of low PSMA avidity as part of therapy assessment for [177Lu]-PSMA radioligand therapy and demonstrates higher sensitivity compared to [68 Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT alone in some patients. Keywords Tot al-body PET · Ultra-long FOV PET · Whole-body PET · PET/CT · Positron-emission-tomography · Digital PET Introduction [177Lu]-labelled PSMA-radioligand therapy (PSMA-RLT) represents a life-prolonging treatment for men with metastatic castration resistance prostate cancer (mCRPC) [1] and is now under investigation for its possible role in metastatic hormone sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC) [2]. Despite the high effi- cacy and low toxicity of [177Lu]-PSMA-RLT [3], selection of patients who stand to benefit from therapy remains challeng- ing. A recent analysis of screen failures from the VISION trial demonstrated poorer outcomes for those classified as ineligible based on baseline PSMA-PET imaging [4], highlighting the importance of adequate theragnostic assessment of patients prior to PSMA-RLT in patient selection and prognostication; patients who exhibit lesions with low PSMA-expression are unlikely to clinically benefit from the procedure. However, what constitutes adequate or optimal assessment is controversial. Whereas the phase II trial published by Hof- man et al. included additional 2-[ 18F]FDG-PET/CT (FDG-PET/ CT) to identify lesions with low PSMA-expression [3], extant EANM procedural guidelines suggest a number of viable imag- ing modalities to identify n

source: context/PSMA/Combined 68 GaGa-PSMA-11 and low-dose 2-18FFDG PETCT using a long-axial field of view scanner for patients referred for 177Lu-PSMA-radioligand therapy.pdf
text: eas the phase II trial published by Hof- man et al. included additional 2-[ 18F]FDG-PET/CT (FDG-PET/ CT) to identify lesions with low PSMA-expression [3], extant EANM procedural guidelines suggest a number of viable imag- ing modalities to identify non-PSMA avid sites of active disease, including bone scan, MRI and contrast-enhanced CT (ceCT) This article is part of the Topical Collection on Oncology - Genitourinary. * Ian Alberts ian.alberts@insel.c h 1 Department of Nuclear Medicine, Inselspital, Bern Univ ersity Hospital, University of Bern, Freiburgstr. 18, 3010 Bern, Switzerland 2 Advanced Clinical Imaging Technology, Siemens Healthcare AG, Lausanne, Switzer land / Published online: 22 September 2022 European Journal of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging (2023) 50:951–956 1 3 as well as FDG-PET/CT [5]. Not all centres routinely perform additional FDG-PET/CT, and reimbursement of an additional PET imaging modality is challenging in many jurisdictions [6]. At present, patients referred for PSMA-RLT have exhausted multiple therapeutic options and are approaching the end-stage of their disease. In this context, the need for a separate imag- ing visit requiring a prolonged fast and journey to a specialist nuclear medicine imaging center represents an additional and potentially unwelcome burden for these patients. Recently introduced long-axial field-of-view (LAFOV) PET/CT scanners demonstrate substantially higher sensitiv- ity compared to previous generation analogue and short- axial FOV systems [7 –11] and which we have previously shown to be useful for [68 Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT at low activities with high diagnostic quality [12]. This higher sen- sitivity can be exploited to afford an additional FDG-PET/ CT with low applied activities (with low equivalent dose, her

source: context/PSMA/Combined 68 GaGa-PSMA-11 and low-dose 2-18FFDG PETCT using a long-axial field of view scanner for patients referred for 177Lu-PSMA-radioligand therapy.pdf
text: previously shown to be useful for [68 Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT at low activities with high diagnostic quality [12]. This higher sen- sitivity can be exploited to afford an additional FDG-PET/ CT with low applied activities (with low equivalent dose, herein referred to as “low-dose”) and as part of the same imaging session. The aim of this short communication is to propose an imaging protocol which combines PSMA and FDG-PET/CT in a single imaging session. Materials and methods Study design This retrospective short communication presents 14 cases who underwent standard clinical [68 Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/ CT on our LAFOV system between January and December 2021, where imaging was performed for therapeutic assess- ment and where additional imaging was performed with a low-dose FDG-PET/CT in the same imaging session. Imaging procedures All scans were performed using the Siemens Biograph Vision Quadra system (axial FOV = 106 cm). R econstruction param- eters were as previously described [12]. Patients first received a PET/CT with [68 Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 using a standard activity (150 ± 15% MBq) at 1 h p.i. with a scan time of 5 min at 1 h [13] and in a single bed position of 106 cm (vertex to thighs). The radiopharmaceutical was prepared as previously published [14]. Immediately upon completion of this scan, 40 MBq of 2-[ 18F]FDG was administered and a second PET/CT performed 1 h thereafter with a 15-min scan time in a single bed posi- tion of 106 cm (vertex to thighs). Reconstruction parameters were as previously described [7, 15]. The total time for the patient which was required for the imaging session therefore took approximately 2 h 15 min. Both scans were performed as clinical routine and patients arrived prepared for the 2-[ 18F] FDG examination in a fasted state (> 4 h), no admini

source: context/PSMA/Combined 68 GaGa-PSMA-11 and low-dose 2-18FFDG PETCT using a long-axial field of view scanner for patients referred for 177Lu-PSMA-radioligand therapy.pdf
text: time for the patient which was required for the imaging session therefore took approximately 2 h 15 min. Both scans were performed as clinical routine and patients arrived prepared for the 2-[ 18F] FDG examination in a fasted state (> 4 h), no adminis tration of insulin in the previous 4 h and with confirmation of a blood sugar < 11.0 mmol/l in accor dance with EANM guidelines [16]. Analysis Both scans were analysed by two qualified nuclear medicine physicians (first and last authors) and mismatch lesions (FDG- avid but with PSMA uptake below the level of the liver) were noted. Semi-quantitative analysis of the images was precluded by the superimposition of the two tracers at the second scan. Results The protocol was implemented in 14 patients undergoing PSMA-RLT at our center during the above mentioned time window. All patients tolerated the protocol well and were com- pliant with instructions for pre-scan fasting. A physiological distribution was noted for the additional low-dose FDG-PET/ CT, with FDG-avid and non-PSMA-accumulating organs such as the brain showing adequate FDG uptake. Subjective image quality was noted to be excellent for both scans. In 13 patients, no mismatch findings could be identified. In n = 1 patient (7%), additional lesions were identified with either absent or low PSMA uptake but high FDG avidity. The following clinical vignettes will demonstrate the fea- sibility and value of the protocol by means of an example patient with mismatch findings and a patient with no mis- match findings. Clinical vignette 1—example patient with mismatch findings This 76-year-old patient was referred for evaluation for PSMA- RLT for mCRPC with known bone and liver metastases. [68 Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT showed multiple bone metas- tases with heterogeneous and low to

source: context/PSMA/Combined 68 GaGa-PSMA-11 and low-dose 2-18FFDG PETCT using a long-axial field of view scanner for patients referred for 177Lu-PSMA-radioligand therapy.pdf
text: nette 1—example patient with mismatch findings This 76-year-old patient was referred for evaluation for PSMA- RLT for mCRPC with known bone and liver metastases. [68 Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT showed multiple bone metas- tases with heterogeneous and low to intense PSMA-expression (SUVmax range 3.2–9.2) and heterogeneous liver metastases, some of which exhibited tracer uptake only partially above the liver background (SUVmax healthy liver 2.9, SUVmax lesion in left liver lobe 4.9). In the following additional FDG-PET/ CT, intense uptake is now seen in the areas of low avidity in the [68 Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT and is demonstrated in the example images in Fig. 1 and the maximum intensity pro- jections (MIP) in Fig. 2. These findings are consistent with lesions with reduced PSMA expression and high FDG avidity. In interdisciplinary discussion with the oncologist and discus- sion with the patient, it was decided not to perform PSMA- RLT for this patient because of the low PSMA avidity of the multiple osseous metastases. 952 European Journal of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging (2023) 50:951–956 1 3 Fig. 1 Example images for a patient wit h mismatch lesions. A and C show the [68 Ga]Ga- PSMA-11 PET/CT performed at 1 h p.i. and B and D the additional PET/CT 1 h follow- ing additional administration of FDG. Although the lesion shown by the broken arrow demonstrates increased uptake at second imaging, the hepatic lesion shown by the solid white arrow in A and the example bone lesion in C show intense FDG uptake at the second PET/ CT (B and D) which is indica- tive of low PSMA expression and high FDG avidity Fig. 2 MIP images f or A [68 Ga] Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT and B FDG of the patient included in Fig. 1 953European Journal of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging (2023) 50:951–956 1 3

text: nd D) which is indica- tive of low PSMA expression and high FDG avidity Fig. 2 MIP images f or A [68 Ga] Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT and B FDG of the patient included in Fig. 1 953European Journal of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging (2023) 50:951–956 1 3 Clinical vignette 2—example patient with no mismatch findings This 81-year-old patient was also referred for evalu- ation of PSMA-RLT for mCRPC. He had received palliative radiotherapy two years previously and was currently receiving leuprolin and abiraterone. The example images from the [68 Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/ CT and FDG-PET/CT shown in Fig. 3 and the MIP in Fig. 4 show no mismatch findings. The patient proceeded to RLT with good response to 6 cycles of [177 Lu]-PSMA-617 (7.4 ± 10% GBq per cycle), wit h a PSA decline > 50% (pre-t herapy 7.7 µg/l, post therapy 1.1 µg/l), with the post-therapy scintigraphy shown also in Fig. 4. Discussion We present a protocol which allows for combined PSMA and FDG-PET/CT imaging within the same session which capitalises on the ability to perform a high-sensitivity and low-dose FDG-PET/CT as part of the same imaging session. The protocol assisted in the identification of one patient who Fig. 3 Example images for patient wit hout mismatch lesions. A and B show images post-[68 Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 PET/T and C and D post- additional PET/CT with FDG. All lesions (lymph nodes, bone, and liver) demonstrate intense uptake in both scans. No new lesions are visible at additional imaging with FDG Fig. 4 Example MIPs of the patient mentioned in F ig. 3 for A [68 Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 and B FDG showing no mismatch lesions. All lesions demon- strated good uptake of [177Lu] Lu-PSMA-617 in the post- therapy scintigraphy (C) 954 European Journal of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging (2023) 50:951–956 1 3 exhibited diss

source: context/PSMA/Combined 68 GaGa-PSMA-11 and low-dose 2-18FFDG PETCT using a long-axial field of view scanner for patients referred for 177Lu-PSMA-radioligand therapy.pdf
text: ]Ga-PSMA-11 and B FDG showing no mismatch lesions. All lesions demon- strated good uptake of [177Lu] Lu-PSMA-617 in the post- therapy scintigraphy (C) 954 European Journal of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging (2023) 50:951–956 1 3 exhibited disseminated bone metastases and a number of liver metastases with low levels of PSMA expression and high FDG avidity, which is consistent with de-differentiated disease and is a relevant consideration in patient selection for PSMA-RLT. We consider that the implementation of an additional low-dose FDG-PET/CT in the same imaging session makes dual-tracer imaging more feasible, with no need for an addi- tional imaging appointment. A combined low-dose proto- col could be more financially viable in centers that do not receive reimbursement for this additional PET/CT exami- nation [6]. Although the lower radiation exposure through lower activities of FDG is of limited value for a patient about to undergo palliative PSMA-RLT, use of lower activities minimises the radiation burden to medical staff and family who must interact with the patient and is consistent with ALARP principles. Moreover, it is our contention that as the providers of a palliative therapy to men in the end-stage of their disease, it is incumbent upon nuclear medicine physi- cians to draw up treatment plans that minimises the burdens placed on patients who are approaching the end of a long and exhaustive medical journey. Fundytus et al. argue that the calls placed upon palliative oncology patients’ limited time is underappreciated in the oncological literature [ 17]. We argue that by combining the additional examination into a single imaging session, not only are we able to better select patients who might benefit from a PSMA-RLT, but can do so in a patient-centered

source: context/PSMA/Combined 68 GaGa-PSMA-11 and low-dose 2-18FFDG PETCT using a long-axial field of view scanner for patients referred for 177Lu-PSMA-radioligand therapy.pdf
text: preciated in the oncological literature [ 17]. We argue that by combining the additional examination into a single imaging session, not only are we able to better select patients who might benefit from a PSMA-RLT, but can do so in a patient-centered and holistic fashion that places value on their individual comfort and time. We note that our protocol was tolerated well by all patients. There are a number of options available to the nuclear medicine physician in designing a dual-tracer imaging pro- tocol, and we consider the protocol described herein to be the best compromise between various competing demands while acknowledging its weaknesses. Ideally, both examina- tions would be performed in a dynamic fashion in order to allow computational extraction of one radiotracers’ uptake compared to another, but we consider the requirement for long-duration dynamic scans to be impracticable for frail patients or in the context of a busy imaging service. An alternative could be to use a low-dose initial PSMA-PET/ CT and to inject a higher activity of the additional FDG, analogous to the same-day combined examination performed as part of myocardial perfusion scintigraphy or combined ventilation/perfusion lung scans with Tc-99 m labelled radi- opharmaceuticals. However, we consider a standard activity initial PSMA-scan to be of the greatest diagnostic value to the patient and caution that further studies are first required to establish the diagnostic acceptability of low-dose PSMA studies [18, 19]. We counsel against the performance of an initial low-dose FDG PET/CT with additional PSMA-PET/ CT, where it would be impossible to differentiate between increasing FDG uptake over time and new foci of PSMA avidity in the second scan [14, 15]. LAFOV systems also offer the possibility to

source: context/PSMA/Combined 68 GaGa-PSMA-11 and low-dose 2-18FFDG PETCT using a long-axial field of view scanner for patients referred for 177Lu-PSMA-radioligand therapy.pdf
text: f an initial low-dose FDG PET/CT with additional PSMA-PET/ CT, where it would be impossible to differentiate between increasing FDG uptake over time and new foci of PSMA avidity in the second scan [14, 15]. LAFOV systems also offer the possibility to reduce patients’ radiation exposure even further with CT-less attenuation correction [20]. There are some limitations to this protocol. We are cognisant of the difficulty of performing semi-quantita - tive assessment of FDG avidity where background PSMA activity is present and where prostate cancer lesions exhibit increasing uptake with time [15, 21]. The impli- cations of this on the suitability of pre-therapy PET/CT for dosimetry will have to be addressed by future stud - ies. We take note of the good FDG uptake in non-PSMA- expressing and FDG-avid organs such as the brain as indicators of acceptable bio-distribution and where iden- tification of mismatch lesions with FDG avidity and low PSMA expression is still possible. The high sensitivity of the scanner and ability to scan for 15 min in a single bed position afforded high-quality FDG images despite the lower injected activity. Future studies might address the optimal imaging protocol for theragnostic assessment of PSMA-RLT. Conclusion We introduce a novel imaging protocol which confirms the feasibility of performing single-session dual-tracer imaging with [68 Ga]Ga-PSMA-11 and FDG for the assessment of patients undergoing PSMA-RLT. source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: ORIGINAL ARTICLE Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT, 18F-sodium fluoride PET/CT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer Helle D. Zacho 1,2 & Julie B. Nielsen 1,2 & Ali Afshar-Oromieh3,4 & Uwe Haberkorn3,5 & Nandita deSouza6 & Katja De Paepe 6 & Katja Dettmann7 & Niels C. Langkilde8 & Christian Haarmark9 & Rune V. Fisker 1,10 & Dennis T. Arp 11 & Jesper Carl12 & Jørgen B. Jensen 7,13 & Lars J. Petersen 1,2 Received: 3 April 2018 / Accepted: 23 May 2018 / Published online: 6 June 2018 # Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2018 Abstract Purpose To prospectively compare diagnostic accuracies for detection of bone metastases by 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT, 18F-NaF PET/CT and diffusion-weighted MRI (DW600-MRI) in prostate cancer (PCa) patients with biochemical recurrence (BCR). Methods Sixty-eight PCa patients with BCR participated in this prospective study. The patients underwent68Ga-PSMA PET/CT, a 18F-NaF PET/CT and a DW600-MRI (performed in accordance with European Society of Urogenital Radiology guidelines, with b values of 0 and 600 s/mm 2). Bone lesions were categorized using a three-point scale (benign, malignant or equivocal for metastases) and a dichotomous scale (benign or metastatic) for each imaging modality by at least two experienced observers. A best valuable comparator was defined for each patient based on study-specific imaging, at least 12 months of clinical follow-up and any imaging prior to the study and during follow-up. Diagnostic performance was assessed using a sensitivity analysis where equivocal lesions were handled as non-metastatic and then as metastatic. Results Ten of the 68 patients were diagnosed with bone metastases. On a patient level, sensitivity, specificit

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: ostic performance was assessed using a sensitivity analysis where equivocal lesions were handled as non-metastatic and then as metastatic. Results Ten of the 68 patients were diagnosed with bone metastases. On a patient level, sensitivity, specificity and the area under the curve (AUC) by receiver operating characteristic analysis were, respectively, 0.80, 0.98–1.00 and 0.89–0.90 for 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT (n = 68 patients); 0.90, 0.90 –0.98 and 0.90 –0.94 for 18NaF PET/CT ( n = 67 patients); and 0.25 –0.38, 0.87–0.92 and 0.59–0.62 for DW 600-MRI (n = 60 patients). The diagnostic performance of DW 600-MRI was significantly lower than that of 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT and 18NaF PET/CT for diagnosing bone metastases ( p < 0.01), and no significant difference in the AUC was seen between 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT and 18NaF PET/CT (p =0 . 6 5 ) . Zacho h.zacho@rn.dk 1 Department of Nuclear Medicine and Clinical Cancer Research Center, Aalborg University Hospital, Hobrovej 18-22, Postboks 365, DK-9100 Aalborg, Denmark 2 Department of Clinical Medicine, Aalborg University, Aalborg, Denmark 3 Department of Nuclear Medicine, University Hospital Heidelberg, Heidelberg, Germany 4 Department of Nuclear Medicine, Bern University Hospital, Bern, Switzerland 5 Clinical Cooperation Unit Nuclear Medicine, DKFZ, Heidelberg, Germany 6 The Institute of Cancer Research and Royal Marsden NHS Foundation Trust, London, UK 7 Department of Urology, Regional Hospital West Jutland, Holstebro, Denmark 8 Department of Urology, Aalborg University Hospital, Aalborg, Denmark 9 Department of Clinical Physiology and Nuclear Medicine,

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: yal Marsden NHS Foundation Trust, London, UK 7 Department of Urology, Regional Hospital West Jutland, Holstebro, Denmark 8 Department of Urology, Aalborg University Hospital, Aalborg, Denmark 9 Department of Clinical Physiology and Nuclear Medicine, Herlev and Gentofte Hospital, Herlev, Denmark 10 Department of Radiology, Aalborg University Hospital, Aalborg, Denmark 11 Department of Medical Physics, Oncology Department, Aalborg University Hospital, Aalborg, Denmark 12 Department of Oncology, Naestved Sygehus, Zealand University Hospital, Roskilde, Denmark 13 Department of Clinical Medicine, Aarhus University, Aarhus, Denmark European Journal of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging (2018) 45:1884–1897 https://doi.org/10.1007/s00259-018-4058-4 Conclusion 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT and 18F-NaF PET/CT showed comparable and high diagnostic accuracies for detecting bone metastases in PCa patients with BCR. Both methods performed significantly better than DW600-MRI, which was inadequate for diagnosing bone metastases when conducted in accordance with European Society of Urogenital Radiology guidelines. Keywords PSMA-PET/CT . NaF-PET/CT . Diffusion-weighted MRI . Bone metastases . Prostate cancer Introduction Biochemical recurrence (BCR) can occur in up to 40% of patients after curatively intended treatment for prostate cancer (PCa) [ 1]. According to the European Association of Urology (EAU) guidelines, bone scintigraphy should not be used in BCR until prostate-specific antigen (PSA) levels reach 10 ng/mL due to the supposed inadequate sen- sitivity of bone scintigraphy when PSA levels are low [ 2, 3]. However, detection of bone metastases even at very low PSA values is of paramount importance because the pres- ence of bone metastases changes the treatment strategy [ 2] and prognosis

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: n- sitivity of bone scintigraphy when PSA levels are low [ 2, 3]. However, detection of bone metastases even at very low PSA values is of paramount importance because the pres- ence of bone metastases changes the treatment strategy [ 2] and prognosis [ 4, 5]. Over the last 5 years, 68Ga-labelled prostate-specific mem- brane antigen ( 68Ga-PSMA) PET/CT has gained widespread use to assess the sites of PCa recurrence [6, 7]. This method is able to identify metastatic lesions in the prostatic bed, lymph nodes, bones and soft tissue in one single investigation at low PSA levels [ 6, 8, 9]. In retrospective studies, 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT shows encouraging diagnostic accuracy for detection of bone metastases compared to bone scintigraphy at the time of staging and in patients with BCR [10]. 18F-sodium fluoride (18F-NaF) PET/CT also evaluates the skeletal system with a high sensitivity and relatively good specificity owing to a high lesion-to-background ratio [ 11, 12]. Finally, MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging (DW-MRI) has been increasingly proposed as a method for visualizing the skeleton without the need for injecting radioactive tracers. Its accuracy in assessing bone has been shown to be high on the basis of early detection of bone marrow lesions [13, 14], although in densely sclerotic lesions, where water content is low and susceptibility effects from calcified bone are present, its interpretation may be more questionable [ 15]. For this reason, European Society of Urogenital Radiology (ESUR) guidelines have recommended lower diffusion weightings in PCa [16] at the cost of reduced diffusion-weighted contrast. Although the usage of the various Bmodern^ imaging mo- dalities has been the subject of trials in patients with advanced PCa [12, 17] and has been assessed for the eva

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: ffusion weightings in PCa [16] at the cost of reduced diffusion-weighted contrast. Although the usage of the various Bmodern^ imaging mo- dalities has been the subject of trials in patients with advanced PCa [12, 17] and has been assessed for the evaluation of treat- ment responses in PCa [ 18], no comparative data between these modalities exist. The purpose of this study was to per- form a prospective comparison of the diagnostic accuracy of 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT, 18NaF PET/CT and DW 600-MRI (the final method applied as per ESUR guidelines) for the detec- tion of bone metastases in PCa patients with BCR. Materials and methods Design A prospective, comparative study of the diagnostic character- istics of 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT, 18NaF PET/CT and DW 600 MRI for the evaluation of bone metastases in patients with BCR after curatively intended treatment of PCa. Patients Patients with BCR after curatively intended treatment of PCa participated in this prospective study of 68Ga PSMA PET/CT (EudraCT no.; 2014-005073-37) [ 19, 20]. Inclusion criteria were: 1) treatment with curative intent for PCa, 2) BCR based on two blood samples at least 2 weeks apart, 3) no ongoing androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) for more than a week before inclusion in the study, and 4) patients were included in the present comparative study of bone metastases if they had undergone at least two of the three index tests ( 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT,18NaF PET/CT and/or a DW600-MRI) for assessment of bone metastases. Consecutive patients from two urology departments were included in the study, and all of the study- related imaging was performed at one site; Aalborg University Hospital. MRI was conducted on the same day as one of the PET/CTs, whereas the order of the PET/CTs were random. BCR was defined as suggested by the EAU g

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: the study, and all of the study- related imaging was performed at one site; Aalborg University Hospital. MRI was conducted on the same day as one of the PET/CTs, whereas the order of the PET/CTs were random. BCR was defined as suggested by the EAU guideline as a PSA level ≥ 0.2 ng/mL after radical prostatectomy, and a PSA level 2 ng/mL greater than the nadir PSA level for patients who had undergone radiation therapy [3]. 68PSMA-PET/CT The PSMA ligand used in the study was 68Ga-PSMA-11, which was synthesized as previously described [ 19]. Approximately 60 min before image acquisition, a bolus in- jection of 2 MBq/kg body weight (minimum 100 MBq, max- imum 200 MBq) was given intravenously. All patients were examined using a VCT Discovery True 64 PET/CT system (GE Healthcare, USA). The scanned area covered the region from the base of the skull to the upper thigh, with 4 min per bed position. PET images were acquired in 3D mode and were reconstructed using attenuation correction, an ordered subset expectation maximization algorithm (128 × 128 matrix, 2 iter- ations, 24 subsets) and a Gauss filter of 6 mm. By default, a Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2018) 45:1884 –1897 1885 low-dose CT was performed immediately after the PET scan. The 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT was compliant with the recent guideline by European Assoc iation of Nuclear Medicine/ Society of Nuclear Medicine on 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT [ 21]; no intravenous furosemide prior to the PET/CT was used in the present study. 18NaF-PET/CT An18NaF PET/CT scan was conducted in accordance with the Society of Nuclear Medicine guideline for 18NaF PET/CT [22]. 18NaF PET/CTs were performed with the same PET/ CT scanner used for 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT. Acquisition was performed 30 min after the intravenous administration of ap- proximately 200 MBq in

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: the Society of Nuclear Medicine guideline for 18NaF PET/CT [22]. 18NaF PET/CTs were performed with the same PET/ CT scanner used for 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT. Acquisition was performed 30 min after the intravenous administration of ap- proximately 200 MBq in 3D mode covering the same anatom- ical area as the 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT, and images were recon- structed using the same parameters. CT Low-dose CT was performed for attenuation correction and anatomical co-registration using the following parameters: 120 kV/10–150 mA and slice thickness 0.625 mm. In six pa- tients, a contrast-enhanced diagnostic CT was performed using the above-mentioned parameters and a maximum of 100 mL (adjusted for kidney function) of Iomeron® 400 mg/mL (Bracco Imaging Scandinavia AB, Göteborg, Sweden). MRI MRI was performed on a 3T MRI scanner (Ingenia 3.0T, Philips Healthcare, Best, The Netherlands) using two anterior torso coils combined with a posterior coil. The combined coils allowed for a coverage of 120 cm, making it possible to scan the patient without moving the coils. The scan sequences followed the 2012 ESUR Prostate MRI Guidelines [ 16] (assessed March 2018) for evaluation of lymph nodes and bone metastases, which suggest using b values of 0 and 600 s/mm 2 (DW600-MRI). The scan field included the pelvis and lumbar and thoracic spine. A minimum number of minor changes to the ESUR protocol were made to fit local proce- dures; parameters for DW 600-MRI, STIR and T1- and T2- weighted sequences are supplied in the supplementary file (Appendix 1). The DW images were subsequently recon- structed as whole-body 3D maximum intensity projection im- ages. Quality assessment of the MRI (including DW 600 im- ages) was conducted after the first two patients and again after completion of the study. Observers a

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: images were subsequently recon- structed as whole-body 3D maximum intensity projection im- ages. Quality assessment of the MRI (including DW 600 im- ages) was conducted after the first two patients and again after completion of the study. Observers and procedure for image assessment The 68Ga-PSMA-PET/CT images were evaluated by three board-certified nuclear medicine physicians; two observers with a high level of experience in 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT and one observer with an intermediate level of experience in PSMA according to the definition by Fendler et al. [ 23]. The evaluation of 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT was conducted according to Rauscher et al. [24]. 18F-NaF PET/CT was evaluated by two board-certified experienced nuclear medicine physicians (one observer with experience from more than 300 18F-NaF PET/ CTs and the other observer with experience from more than 2000 18F-NaF PET/CTs). For the evaluation of the 18F-NaF PET/CT, areas with increased 18F-NaF-uptake were assessed and the low-dose CT was used for anatomical co-registration; e.g. increased 18NaF uptake located around joints or in degen- erative changes was considered benign. Finally, DW600-MRI was evaluated together with T1-weighted images and fat- suppressed T2-images by two boa rd-certified radiologists with at least 2 years of experience in reading whole-body DW-MRIs. Lesions hyperintense on DW 600-MRI, correlating with hyper-intense lesions on STIR and hypo-intense lesions on T1, were considered malignant. For each of the imaging modalities, the observers were asked to categorize the patient using a three-point-scale: bone metastases present (M1), no bone metastases (M0) or equivo- cal in patients with bone lesions not typical for bone metasta- ses. Additionally, observers provided a dichotomous answer (M0 or M1) on a

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: gorize the patient using a three-point-scale: bone metastases present (M1), no bone metastases (M0) or equivo- cal in patients with bone lesions not typical for bone metasta- ses. Additionally, observers provided a dichotomous answer (M0 or M1) on a patient level (secondary endpoint). Finally, the exact number and location of all metastatic lesions were described (in patients with more than 10 lesions, the exact localizations were not described). The observers had no access to the other imaging modality results or clinical information except for the PCa diagnosis with BCR after curatively intended treatment. In general, the observers evaluated the images indepen- dently, then the individual evaluations within each imaging modality were compared. In cases of disagreement (either in the categorization or in the number of lesions), the readers re- evaluated the scans, and any disagreement was resolved by consensus. Best valuable comparator A histopathologic reference standard is rare, and frequently, is it not feasible to obtain in imaging of bone metastases for ethical reasons. Accordingly, a Bbest valuable comparator ^ (BVC) was defined for the presence or absence of bone me- tastases on a patient level. A BVC has been used in previous diagnostic studies of bone metastases [ 10, 11, 13, 25]. Moreover, any lesion described by at least one imaging mo- dality was assessed to provide a BVC on a lesion level. The BVC was based on all available imaging —previous, study-related imaging, supplementary imaging and follow-up imaging ( 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT, 18F-NaF PET/CT, 18F-FDG PET/CT, anatomical MRI, DW 600-MRI, contrast-enhanced CT and bone scintigraphy), at least 12 months of clinical 1886 Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2018) 45:1884 –1897 follow-up and routine blood samples. Clinic

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT, 18F-NaF PET/CT, 18F-FDG PET/CT, anatomical MRI, DW 600-MRI, contrast-enhanced CT and bone scintigraphy), at least 12 months of clinical 1886 Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2018) 45:1884 –1897 follow-up and routine blood samples. Clinical data were re- trieved from the case report f orms and medical reports. Patients who received localized treatment such as salvage ra- diotherapy of the prostatic bed or lymph node dissection with- out any systemic anticancer treatment and subsequently had unmeasurable PSA levels (defined as < 0.1 ng/mL) were con- sidered as being Btrue^ negative for bone metastases. Physicians participating in the study (two nuclear medicine physicians, one radiologist and one urologist) from one site (Aalborg) evaluated the available information to define whether bone metastases were present, as well as the number and location of bone metastases. The BVC conclusions were accepted by all institutions. Statistics The results are summarized by their mean or median values, standard deviations and ranges. Patient-based anal- yses was performed using the BVC as the reference test. For each imaging modality, the sensitivity, specificity, pos- itive predictive value (PPV) and negative predictive value (NPV) were calculated using data from all of the patients scanned by each modality. Performance of the methods was determined using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves; areas under the curve (AUCs) and 95% confidence intervals (95% CIs) for the AUCs were calcu- lated. Sensitivity analyses were performed with equivocal lesions handled as non-metastatic ( Boptimistic ^ analysis) and with equivocal lesions handled as metastatic (Bpessimistic ^ analysis). The AUCs for the three modali- ties were recomputed using only those patients investigat

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: performed with equivocal lesions handled as non-metastatic ( Boptimistic ^ analysis) and with equivocal lesions handled as metastatic (Bpessimistic ^ analysis). The AUCs for the three modali- ties were recomputed using only those patients investigat- ed by all modalities and compared using a non-parametric test as suggested by DeLong et al. [ 26]. A simple logistic regression with equivocal scans (yes/no) as the outcome and modality as the explanatory variable was used to in- vestigate whether some modalities produced more equivo- cal findings than others. Statistical analyses were per- formed using STA TA®11 (StataCorp LP , College Station, TX, USA). All results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Tests performed were two-sided and the significance level was set to 0.05. Ethics This study complied with the Helsinki II Declaration. All pa- tients provided signed, informed consent to participate. The study protocol was approved by the North Denmark Region Committee on Health Research Ethics (N-20140080), the Danish Health and Medicine Authority and the Danish Data Protection Agency. The trial was registered in the EudraCT database (EudraCT no.; 2014 –005073-37). In addition, the study was monitored by the Good Clinical Practice (GCP) unit at Aalborg and Aarhus University Hospitals, Denmark. Results Patients Of the 69 patients included in this study, 1 was found to have lymphoma at the time of the study and consequently excluded from the analysis. The median PSA level at the time of the study was 0.4 ng/mL, and 65% of the patients (44/68) had a PSA level ≤ 1.0 ng/mL. Table 1 summarizes data for each treatment group. Sixty-six patients did not receive ADT for at least 6 months before the study or during the study, one patient had ADT for 1 week before study

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: 65% of the patients (44/68) had a PSA level ≤ 1.0 ng/mL. Table 1 summarizes data for each treatment group. Sixty-six patients did not receive ADT for at least 6 months before the study or during the study, one patient had ADT for 1 week before study start and one patient received a waiver for inclusion due to 2 weeks of ADT treatment. All patients were investigated by a 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT scan (n = 68), 67 patients underwent a 18NaF PET/CT scan (1 patient declined to travel twice for the scans) and 60 patients had a DW600-MRI scan (the predominant reason for avoiding MRI was metal implants that were not compatible with MRI). The median time between the three investigations was 4 days, with 90% being done within 10 days (range 1 to 47 days). A final consensus diagnosis of bone metastases was reached for every patient on the three-point scale and on the dichotomous scale by the observers for each of the study- related imaging modalities. Best valuable comparator Patients It was possible to determine the presence or absence of bone metastasis in all patients (Fig. 1). A total of 10 patients (15%) with a median PSA 3.0 ng/mL were diagnosed with bone me- tastases (Table2); 58 patients with a median PSA of 0.4 ng/mL did not have bone metastases (Fig.1,T a b l e3). No patients who were diagnosed as M0 by the BVC at the time of the study developed bone metastases according to available follow-up imaging and clinical follow-up over 12 months. Lesions The number of metastatic bone lesions was evaluable in 9 of 10 patients categorized as M1 by the BVC, and a total of 16 lesions were diagnosed as bone metastases; one patient pre- sented with 10+ rib lesions that were too numerous to evaluate. Sixteen imaging-avid lesions (by at least one of the index tests) were classified as benign

text: M1 by the BVC, and a total of 16 lesions were diagnosed as bone metastases; one patient pre- sented with 10+ rib lesions that were too numerous to evaluate. Sixteen imaging-avid lesions (by at least one of the index tests) were classified as benign (details are provided in Table3). Patient-based analysis 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT indicated bone metastases in 8 (12%) patients and equivocal findings in one patient (1%). 68Ga- Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2018) 45:1884 –1897 1887 PSMA PET/CT correctly identified 8/10 patients with con- firmed bone metastases, with no false positive patients (Table 4). 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT was false negative in two patients (Table 2, patients 1 and 4). Two patients began ADT shortly before entering the study; none showed patho- logic 68Ga-PSMA uptake (both were declared M0 by the BVC). In addition, 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT showed extra- skeletal lesions suspicious of PCa metastases in 32 patients (data not shown). 18 F-NaF PET/CT diagnosed bone metastasis in 10 (15%) patients, whereas 5 (7%) patients were equivocal for bone metastases. 18F-NaF PET/CT correctly identified 9/10 patients with bone metastases (Table 4). One patient had false positive results for bone metastases (Table 3, patient 11), and one patient had false negative results (Table 2, patient 3). All patients reported as equivocal by 18F-NaF PET/CT or by 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT were classi- fied as M0 using the dichotomous scale. According to the consensus diagnosis for DW 600-MRI, bone metastases were seen in six patients (10%), and four patients were equivocal for bone metastases (7%). Compared to the BVC-based results, DW 600-MRI correctly identified two of eight patients with bone metastases; four patients had false positive results (Table 4). The scan field for DW600-MRI included only the pelvis and the lumbar and thoracic spine; two patients had a single bone metastasis outside the area covered by DW 600-MRI according to the BVC, and an analysis taking the field of view into ac- count was performed (Table 4). A thorough evaluation of the patients with false positive results is summarized in Table3. Four patients were deemed equivocal by DW600-MRI, and using the dichotomous scale, one patient was categorized as M0 and three as M1. Compared to the BVC-based categori- zations, this resulted in one patient with a false negative result (Table 2, patient 10) and three with false positive results (Table 3, patients 12, 13 and 16). Of the 59 patients investigated by all modalities, uniform agreement for M0 was seen in 43 patients (73%), who were all classified as M0 by the BVC. Uniform agreement for M1 was seen in two patients (3.3%), with both classified as M1 by the BVC (Fig. 2). In two other

text: s investigated by all modalities, uniform agreement for M0 was seen in 43 patients (73%), who were all classified as M0 by the BVC. Uniform agreement for M1 was seen in two patients (3.3%), with both classified as M1 by the BVC (Fig. 2). In two other patients (3.3%), a bone metastasis was seen outside the DW 600-MRI scanning field. Finally, con- flicting categorizations from the three modalities were seen in 12 patients (20%), of whom 5 were M1 (Table 2,F i g .3)a n d seven were M0 according to the BVC (Table 3). Accuracy of the imaging modalities, as calculated according to the AUC in the ROC analysis ( n = 59), showed significant differences [AUC (95% CI): 68 Ga-PSMA-PET/CT, 1.0 (1.0 –1.0); 18NaF-PET/CT, 0.93 (0.80 –1.0); and DW 600-MRI. 0.57 (0.40–0.73); p < 0.001]. When patients with a bone metastasis outside the DW 600-MRI scanning field were excluded, the accuracy, as calculated by the ROC analysis, remained signif- icantly lower for DW 600-MRI than for PET/CT. This was also the case for both the Boptimistic^ and Bpessimistic^ analyses of equivocal patients. No statistically significant difference ( p = 0.65) was seen between 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT and 18NaF PET/CT for the de- tection of bone metastases as measured by the ROC analysis (n = 67); the AUC (95% CI) for 68Ga-PSMA-PET/CT was 0.90 (0.77–1.0), and for 18NaF-PET/CT, it was 0.94 (0.84 – 1.0). There was no significant difference in the proportion of equivocal results between the modalities (p =0 . 1 7 ) . Additionally, DW600-MRI categorized one patient with diffuse metastatic involvement of the skeleton— BVC deemed the patient to be M0 (patient 7, Table 3). The patient with 10+ metastases according to the BVC had two metastases in the scapula and at least 5 certain metastases in the ribs by 68Ga PSMA PET/CT, and 18NaF PET/CT diag- nosed the two scapular metastases and 10+ rib metastases. DW 600-MRI also categorized this patient as M1, but based on completely different lesions in the spine that were not seen with the two other modalities. The lesions in the spine were non-metastatic according to the BVC. Discussion The present comparative is, to the best of our knowledge, the first prospective study to dire ctly compare the diagnostic properties of 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT to those of 18NaF PET/ CT and DW 600-MRI in assessing bone metastases using a homogeneous group of patients with BCR and low PSA levels. A key finding was an excellent diagnostic accuracy for the assessment of bone metastases by both 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT and 18NaF PET/CT, but significantly worse diagnos- tic characteristics of DW 600-MRI performed according to ESUR guidelines for bone lesions. 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT has previously shown excellent de- tection rates in patients with BCR [6], but only a single study has evaluated 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT specifically for bones [10, 27]. Pyka et al. demonstrated a sensitivity of 95 –99% and a specificity of 88–98% with optimistic and pessimistic analy- ses for the evaluation of bone metastases in BCR on a patient level [10]. Their specifici

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: SMA PET/CT specifically for bones [10, 27]. Pyka et al. demonstrated a sensitivity of 95 –99% and a specificity of 88–98% with optimistic and pessimistic analy- ses for the evaluation of bone metastases in BCR on a patient level [10]. Their specificity was comparable to our findings, whereas their sensitivity was slightly higher. The difference in sensitivity may be PET -related; we used a relatively old PET scanner without a time-of-flight or point spread function in our PET/CT imaging, which is known to increase the signal- to-noise ratio and image quality [ 28] and has been applied in previous studies [10]. The high sensitivity of 18NaF PET/CT has been shown in other studies of bone metastases in PCa patients [ 14, 29], in populations with mixed cancers [ 11] and in systematic re- views [30]. These studies reported somewhat lower specific- ities, from 54% in the assessment of spine metastases when compared to MRI [ 29] to specificities of approximately 70 – 80% [31, 32]. Our sensitivity is in line with data in the existing literature, though we demonstrate a high specificity, which is similar to a recent study comparing 18NaF PET/CT to bone scintigraphy by Löfgren et al. [ 11]. In contrast, DW 600-MRI exhibited a low sensitivity and modest specificity. The diagnostic accuracy of DW 600-MRI was significantly lower than that of 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT and 18NaF PET/CT and was much lower than reported in previous studies applying DW-MRI for the assessment of bone metastases [13, 14]. Exclusion of the two patients with bone metastases outside the DW 600-MRI

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: antly lower than that of 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT and 18NaF PET/CT and was much lower than reported in previous studies applying DW-MRI for the assessment of bone metastases [13, 14]. Exclusion of the two patients with bone metastases outside the DW 600-MRI scanning field did not eliminate the significant difference between DW600−MRI and PET/CT. In particular, a high number of false positive findings was seen. A thorough evaluation and follow up of the patients with false positive results revealed no clinical signs of bone metastases or metastases according to additional imaging despite the fact that none of the patients received any anticancer treatment during the follow-up period. The diagnostic performance of DW 600-MRI was low when compared to results from previous studies evaluating DW- MRI for the detection of bone metastases [ 13, 14, 33, 34] and likely reflects the choice of diffusion weighting. The ac- quisition parameters in the present study were based on the latest guideline by ESUR for lymph nodes and bone metasta- ses at the time of protocol development [16] in order to attain standardized settings; the guideline remains the official guide- line from a radiologic society today. The low b value is likely to account for the low sensitivity (33%) of our results. The b value of 600 used by Nakanishi et al. [ 34] in a study of 30 patients, of whom only 17 had PCa, reported a sensitivity of 98%, and the results reflect a different population largely with- out sclerotic metastases. The use of DW-MRI for tumour staging in primary PCa has been strongly debated owing to challenges with image acqui- sition, quality and interpretation standardization, and some of these challenges have been partly addressed by the ESUR guidelines [16], which have recently been expanded [ 35].

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: ary PCa has been strongly debated owing to challenges with image acqui- sition, quality and interpretation standardization, and some of these challenges have been partly addressed by the ESUR guidelines [16], which have recently been expanded [ 35]. In the present study, the observers analysing the DW 600-MRI images were experienced in interpreting DW-MRI images, but the lack of a high b value to provide sufficient diffusion- weighted contrast resulted in discrepancies in readings that required a consensus resolution in a third of the cases. Review and appropriate revision of the ESUR guideline is therefore warranted. Moreover, the imaging acquisition was done at a separate center without input from the radiologists reading the images into the data acquisition parameters, nor was there any ongoing quality control of the images obtained. Setting up DW-MRI sequence collection for assessing PCa requires more control of the image acquisition methodolo- gy than does setting up PSMA PET/CT or 18F-NaF PET/ CT from scratch. Since the first reports on 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT in 2012 [36, 37], its use has been implemented in numerous institutions across Europe and Asia, whereas the use of whole-body DW-MRI for assessments of bone metastases has been confined to few dedicated centers over the same time span because strict quality assurance and quality control measures are essential to achieve data that can be interpreted meaningfully. a b f c ed Fig. 2 Example of a patient with bone metastases in the pelvis according to the study-related modalities and the BVC (PSA 5.2 ng/mL at the time of the study, BCR after prostatectomy, Table 2, patient 2): one bone metastases is present in the left pubic bone and two in the right pubic bone (in the field of view shown). There was excellent concordance on a patient level by the three study-related modalities. 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT (a) shows markedly increased uptake in the posterior lesion in the right pubic bone (arrow) but no increased uptake in the other metastases. 18F- NaF PET/CT shows increased uptake in all metastases ( arrows). The corresponding CT image ( c) shows the mixed sclerotic/osteolytic bone metastases (arrows). On DW 600-MRI, only the lesion in the right pubic bone was detected as a focal high-intensity lesion ( arrow) on the native diffusion-weighted (b600) image (d), correlating with a low signal on the associated apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) map ( arrow ; e), confirming this is indeed a bone metastasis. The metastases in the left pubic bone were not detected. The corresponding anatomical MRI, T2- weighted image (f) shows the three bone metastases (arrows) Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging (2018) 45:1884 –1897 1893 The strengths of the present study lie in the prospective design with its inherent data completeness. The population constituted a very homogeneous group of patients with BCR after curatively intended treatment of PCa, with more than two thirds of the patients having PSA levels < 1 ng/mL. Moreover, the majority of PSMA PET/CT studies in patients with BCR have included patients receiving ADT. Currently, the impact of ADT on PSMA-ligand PET/CT has not been finally eval- uated. The vast majority of patients in this study did not re- ceive ADT. A BVC was chosen as the reference standard as bone bi- opsy of every lesion seen in any of the investig

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: ntly, the impact of ADT on PSMA-ligand PET/CT has not been finally eval- uated. The vast majority of patients in this study did not re- ceive ADT. A BVC was chosen as the reference standard as bone bi- opsy of every lesion seen in any of the investigations was not acceptable for ethical and logistical reasons, and random bone biopsies in patients without lesions to verify the lack of me- tastases were also not warranted. BVCs have been applied in similar studies previously [ 10, 11, 13], but it is of utmost importance to keep the intrinsic verification bias in mind as only lesions seen by the index tests can be properly evaluated [12]. We strived to render the decisions behind determining the BVC transparent (Fig. 1, Tables 2 and 3). Eleven patients experienced a fall in PSA level to < 0.1 ng/mL following local treatment of the prostatic bed or lymph node dissection and were consequently diagnosed without bone metastases. In all but five patients (Fig.1), the BVC was based on two different anatomical modalities (CT and anatomical MRI), and at least two of the three different functional imag- ing modalities provide information about the bones; PSMA (which is specifically bound to the cellular membrane in PCa- cells); NaF (which is a taken up in areas with increased bone turnover, thus indicating the bone reaction secondary to me- tastasis); and DW-MRI (which depends on the random motion of water molecules, enabling detection of bone metastases before any bone remodelling has occurred) [13]. According to the European guidelines, bone scintigra- phy should not be performed in patients with BCR until the PSA level reaches 10 ng/mL [ 3]. In the present study, we mostly included (> 90%) patients with a PSA level ≤ 1.0 ng/mL

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: s occurred) [13]. According to the European guidelines, bone scintigra- phy should not be performed in patients with BCR until the PSA level reaches 10 ng/mL [ 3]. In the present study, we mostly included (> 90%) patients with a PSA level ≤ 1.0 ng/mL, and 15% of the patients had bone metastases according to the BVC. This finding is in line with previ- ous retrospective studies [ 38]. Calais et al. reported 23 patients (9%) with bone metastases according to 68Ga- PSMA PET/CT among 270 patients with BCR and a PSA level ≤ 1.0 ng/mL after radical prostatectomy [ 9]. 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT has the inherent advantage of visu- alizing local recurrence, as well as lymph node and soft tissue metastases [ 39]. A complete sensitivity analysis on a lesion level was not performed since the small number of patients with a large number of lesions would strongly affect the analysis; this is because defining the number of true negative lesions is com- plex and because treatment with curative intent is decided at a patient level rather than at a lesion level. This comparative study assessed three novel imaging meth- odologies for the assessment of bone lesion at BCR. Similar comparative studies are warranted in other settings, e.g., stag- ing in high-risk patients and for the assessment of treatment responses in metastatic castration-resistant PCa. We know from recent studies that apparent similar methods like bone scans and 18NaF PET/CT may show conflicting data for the assessment of treatment responses in PCa [ 40]. Conclusion In the present prospective head-to-head comparison of 68Ga- PSMA PET/CT, 18NaF PET/CT and DW600-MRI in PCa pa- tients with BCR, both 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT and 18NaF PET/ CT showed comparable and high diagnostic accuracies for the detection of bone metastases and performed s

source: context/PSMA/Prospective comparison of 68Ga-PSMA PETCT, 18F-sodium fluoride PETCT and diffusion weighted-MRI at for the detection of bone metastases in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer.pdf
text: pective head-to-head comparison of 68Ga- PSMA PET/CT, 18NaF PET/CT and DW600-MRI in PCa pa- tients with BCR, both 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT and 18NaF PET/ CT showed comparable and high diagnostic accuracies for the detection of bone metastases and performed significantly bet- ter than DW 600-MRI, where the diffusion-weighted contrast, as recommended by the ESUR, was inadequate to distinguish bone metastases. 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT has the advantage over 18NaF PET/CT of visualizing bone as well as soft-tissue le- sions and thus should be used for localization of metastases in patients with BCR.
